title: Legislation relating to the environment
original_text: Parliament shall enact legislation to give full effect to the provisions of this Part.
what_this_means: Parliament must pass laws to make the environmental protections in the Constitution real and enforceable — not leave them as aspirational words. Environmental legislation must give practical teeth to the rights and obligations set out in this chapter.
examples: Citizens notice that there are no clear legal penalties for companies that violate environmental rules because Parliament has not yet passed detailed environmental enforcement legislation. Under this article, Parliament has a constitutional duty to fill that gap.

A court is asked to enforce an environmental right but finds no legislation specifying how to do so. This triggers Parliament's obligation under this article — environmental rights need enabling legislation to be fully effective.

title: Responsibilities of leadership
original_text: Authority assigned to a State officer is a public trust to be exercised consistently with the Constitution, demonstrating respect for the people, bringing honour to the nation, and promoting public confidence in the integrity of the office. State officers shall serve the people, not rule them. Leadership principles include personal integrity, objectivity, selfless service, accountability to the public, and commitment in service.
what_this_means: Every State officer — from the President to a ward administrator — holds public power as a trust on behalf of citizens, not as their personal property. Their job is to serve, not to dominate. Corruption, favouritism, and self-serving behaviour all betray this constitutional trust.
examples: A cabinet secretary uses government vehicles and staff to campaign for their political party during working hours. This is a misuse of public authority — it serves their personal political interest rather than the people, violating the leadership responsibilities in this article.

A county official awards all government contracts to members of his ethnic community, regardless of merit. This violates the constitutional requirement that leaders be objective, impartial, and not influenced by nepotism — it is a betrayal of public trust.

title: Oath of office of State officers
original_text: Before assuming a State office, a person shall take and subscribe to the oath or affirmation of office in the manner and form prescribed by the Third Schedule or under an Act of Parliament.
what_this_means: Every State officer — including MPs, judges, the President — must swear an oath or make a formal affirmation before they can exercise any power. Taking that oath is a solemn commitment to serve the Constitution and the people of Kenya.
examples: A newly elected county governor begins issuing directives and signing official documents before being sworn in. Any actions taken before the oath of office are constitutionally invalid — the oath is a prerequisite to lawfully exercising State power.

An MP refuses to take the prescribed oath, insisting on his own version. The Constitution is specific about the form of the oath — an MP who does not take it as required cannot lawfully sit or vote in Parliament.

title: Conduct of State officers
original_text: A State officer shall behave in a manner that avoids conflict between personal interests and public duties, compromising public interest for personal benefit, or demeaning the office held. Violation may result in dismissal. A person dismissed for these violations is disqualified from holding any other State office.
what_this_means: State officers cannot put their personal financial interests or private agenda above their public duties. If caught doing so, they can be dismissed and permanently barred from ever holding any other State office.
examples: A county executive committee member awards a supply contract to her own company while chairing the procurement meeting. This is a direct conflict between her personal financial interest and her public duty — it is grounds for dismissal under this article.

A Principal Secretary who is dismissed for misconduct applies for appointment as a commissioner a year later. Because the dismissal was for the conduct described in this article, he is constitutionally disqualified from holding any other State office.

title: Financial probity of State officers
original_text: A gift or donation to a State officer on a public or official occasion is a gift to the Republic and must be delivered to the State. A State officer shall not maintain a bank account outside Kenya except in accordance with an Act of Parliament, or seek or accept a personal loan or benefit that compromises their integrity.
what_this_means: Gifts given to government officials at official functions belong to Kenya, not to the official personally. State officers also cannot keep bank accounts abroad or accept loans and benefits that create obligations or compromises to their public duties.
examples: A foreign dignitary presents a valuable painting to a Cabinet Secretary during a State function. The painting belongs to the Republic of Kenya — the Cabinet Secretary must hand it over to the State and cannot keep it at home as a personal gift.

A governor accepts a private loan from a company that regularly bids for county tenders. Even if repaid, this creates a conflict of interest that compromises his integrity — it is a violation of this article and grounds for disciplinary action.

title: Restriction on activities of State officers
original_text: A full-time State officer shall not participate in any other gainful employment. Any appointed State officer shall not hold office in a political party. A retired State officer receiving a pension shall not hold more than two concurrent remunerative positions in State companies or organs.
what_this_means: Full-time government officers cannot hold other paid jobs — their public duty demands their full attention. Appointed officers must stay out of party politics. And retired officers on a government pension cannot take on too many other paid government roles.
examples: A Principal Secretary quietly maintains a paid directorship in a private company while serving in government. This is prohibited — full-time State officers cannot participate in other gainful employment while in office.

A retired judge receiving a government pension sits as the paid chairperson of three different State corporations simultaneously. The Constitution limits retired officers receiving a pension to a maximum of two concurrent remunerative positions in State entities.

title: Citizenship and leadership
original_text: A person is not eligible for election or appointment to a State office unless they are a citizen of Kenya. A State officer or member of the defence forces shall not hold dual citizenship, unless they were made a citizen of another country by operation of that country's law without ability to opt out.
what_this_means: You must be a Kenyan citizen to hold any State office. And if you are already a State officer or in the military, you cannot hold citizenship of another country at the same time — unless you were given it automatically by another country's law and had no choice.
examples: A person who recently became a naturalised Kenyan citizen after living in Kenya for years wants to run for a county assembly seat. Provided they are a citizen at the time of the election, they are eligible — citizenship is the key requirement.

An army officer discovers he automatically became a citizen of a foreign country by descent following a change in that country's citizenship law, without any action on his part. The Constitution exempts cases where citizenship was acquired by operation of another country's law without ability to opt out — his position is protected.

title: Legislation to establish the ethics and anti-corruption commission
original_text: Parliament shall enact legislation to establish an independent ethics and anti-corruption commission for purposes of ensuring compliance with, and enforcement of, the provisions of this Chapter.
what_this_means: The Constitution requires Parliament to create an independent ethics and anti-corruption body to enforce the leadership and integrity rules. This commission must be independent — it cannot be controlled by the politicians it is supposed to watch.
examples: A politician is accused of misusing public funds and the public wants to know why the anti-corruption commission has not investigated. The Constitution mandates Parliament to establish this commission with real independence and enforcement powers — citizens can demand it be functional and resourced.

The government attempts to put the ethics commission under the control of the Office of the President, making it report to the President rather than Parliament. This undermines its constitutional independence — the commission must be independent to enforce integrity rules against all State officers.

title: Legislation on leadership
original_text: Parliament shall enact legislation establishing procedures and mechanisms for administering this Chapter, prescribing additional penalties, providing for application to public officers, and making other necessary provisions for promoting leadership principles and their enforcement.
what_this_means: Parliament must pass laws to make the Chapter 6 leadership and integrity requirements enforceable — including clear penalties for breaches that go beyond what the Constitution already spells out.
examples: A county official breaches the integrity rules but there is no clear legal penalty because Parliament has not yet enacted the required legislation. This highlights Parliament's constitutional duty — without it, enforcement is weakened and violators may escape accountability.

A civil society organisation petitions Parliament to speed up passing of integrity laws affecting public officers below State officer level. Article 80 explicitly requires Parliament to extend these protections to public officers generally — not just top State officials.

title: General principles for the electoral system
original_text: The electoral system shall comply with the following principles: freedom of citizens to exercise political rights, not more than two-thirds of elective public bodies shall be of the same gender, fair representation of persons with disabilities, universal suffrage based on fair representation and equality of vote, and free and fair elections by secret ballot, free from violence and corruption, conducted by an independent body, transparently and impartially.
what_this_means: Kenya's elections must be genuinely free and fair — your vote must be secret, the process must be transparent, and no single gender can dominate elective bodies. If those standards are not met, the election can be challenged in court.
examples: Party agents at a polling station are allowed inside the voting booth area, compromising the secrecy of the ballot. This directly violates the constitutional requirement for a secret ballot — the election results in that station can be challenged.

A county assembly is elected with 68% of members being male. This exceeds the two-thirds gender rule — the result violates the constitutional principle and the IEBC must take corrective measures through the party list system.

title: Legislation on elections
original_text: Parliament shall enact legislation providing for delimitation of electoral units, nomination of candidates, continuous registration of voters, conduct of elections and referenda, and progressive registration of citizens residing outside Kenya.
what_this_means: Parliament must pass laws covering every aspect of elections — from how constituencies are drawn to how Kenyans in the diaspora can vote. These are not optional — if Parliament fails to enact them, it can be compelled to do so.
examples: Kenyan citizens living and working in Australia want to vote in the general election but no system exists for diaspora voting. The Constitution requires Parliament to progressively implement their right to vote — the delay in creating that system is a failure of a constitutional obligation.

A candidate is nominated by a party under unclear procedures that were never defined in law. Because Parliament is required to enact clear nomination laws, the lack of clear rules can be grounds for a legal challenge to the nomination process.

title: Registration as a voter
original_text: A person qualifies for registration as a voter if they are an adult citizen, not declared to be of unsound mind, and have not been convicted of an election offence during the preceding five years. A citizen who qualifies shall be registered at only one centre. Administrative arrangements shall not deny an eligible citizen the right to vote.
what_this_means: Every adult Kenyan citizen who is mentally sound and has not committed an election offence in the last five years has the right to be registered as a voter. No administrative barriers — long queues, inconvenient locations, complicated processes — can be used to effectively deny eligible citizens that right.
examples: The IEBC sets up voter registration centres only in urban areas, making it impossible for millions of rural Kenyans to register. The Constitution requires administrative arrangements to facilitate voter registration, not obstruct it — this arrangement can be legally challenged.

A person who was convicted of voter bribery three years ago attempts to register as a voter. She is disqualified — election offences within the preceding five years are one of the specific grounds for disqualification from registration.

title: Candidates for election and political parties to comply with code of conduct
original_text: In every election, all candidates and all political parties shall comply with the code of conduct prescribed by the Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission.
what_this_means: No candidate or political party is above the IEBC's election rules. They must all follow the code of conduct — which covers campaigning ethics, prohibited conduct, and the handling of electoral materials.
examples: A powerful political party ignores the IEBC code of conduct by campaigning in school compounds and distributing cash to voters. The code is constitutionally binding — the IEBC can take action against both the party and its candidates for violating it.

An independent candidate uses hate speech in his campaign materials, which violates the IEBC code of conduct. The IEBC has authority to disqualify candidates who breach the code — compliance is not optional for anyone seeking election.

title: Eligibility to stand as an independent candidate
original_text: Any person is eligible to stand as an independent candidate if they are not a member of a registered political party and have not been a member for at least three months immediately before the date of the election.
what_this_means: You can run for office without belonging to a political party — but you must have genuinely left any party at least three months before the election. You cannot leave a party a week before the elections and claim to be an independent candidate.
examples: A popular community leader resigns from her political party two months before the election deadline and tries to register as an independent candidate. She is not eligible — the Constitution requires at least three months of non-membership before the election.

A young man who has never joined any political party decides to run for his ward's county assembly seat as an independent candidate. He meets the eligibility requirement — he is not and has never been a member of a registered party.

title: Voting
original_text: At every election, the IEBC shall ensure that the voting system is simple, accurate, verifiable, secure, accountable and transparent; votes are counted and results announced promptly at each polling station; results are openly collated and announced by the returning officer; and appropriate structures are in place to eliminate electoral malpractice.
what_this_means: The IEBC must run elections that are technically sound, transparent, and quick. Results must be announced promptly at each polling station — not delayed or hidden. The process from voting to final announcement must be fully observable and verifiable.
examples: After polls close, returning officers in several constituencies delay announcing results for days while handling ballot boxes in secret locations. The Constitution requires results to be announced promptly and the collation to be open — this delay and secrecy violates the law.

The IEBC introduces a new electronic voting system that cannot be independently verified and produces no paper trail. The Constitution requires the system to be verifiable and transparent — a system that cannot be audited fails this constitutional standard.

title: Electoral disputes
original_text: Parliament shall enact legislation to establish mechanisms for timely settling of electoral disputes. Petitions concerning an election other than a presidential election shall be filed within 28 days after the declaration of results.
what_this_means: If you believe an election was flawed, you have exactly 28 days from the announcement of results to file a petition in court. Miss that window and you lose your right to challenge the results, no matter how strong your evidence.
examples: Atieno believes the National Assembly election in her constituency was stolen through ballot stuffing. She has 28 days from the day the IEBC declared the winner to file an election petition in the High Court — after that, her case cannot be heard.

A voter who witnessed irregularities waits two months before trying to file an election petition, thinking the 28-day period is just a guideline. The court dismisses his case immediately — the 28-day window is a constitutional deadline, not a suggestion.

title: Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission
original_text: There is established the Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission. It is responsible for conducting or supervising referenda and elections, continuous voter registration, revision of the voters' roll, delimitation of constituencies and wards, regulation of party nominations, settlement of certain electoral disputes, voter education, and monitoring election funding.
what_this_means: The IEBC runs Kenya's elections — independently, without interference from government or political parties. It also draws constituency boundaries, educates voters, and resolves disputes before results are declared. Its independence is constitutionally guaranteed.
examples: The President issues a directive instructing the IEBC to postpone the election by two months. The IEBC is constitutionally independent — the President has no authority to give it directions on how to run elections, and the commission can refuse and proceed.

A political party claims the IEBC unfairly redrew constituency boundaries to disadvantage them. The IEBC's boundary delimitation process must follow constitutional criteria and can be challenged in the High Court within 30 days of publication.

title: Delimitation of electoral units
original_text: There shall be 290 constituencies. The IEBC shall review names and boundaries of constituencies at intervals of not less than 8 years and not more than 12 years, with review completed at least 12 months before a general election. Boundaries shall reflect population quotas, with margins of 40% for cities and sparse areas and 30% for others.
what_this_means: Kenya has 290 constituencies and the boundaries must be reviewed regularly to reflect population changes. No review can happen within 12 months of an election — and the boundaries must ensure each constituency represents roughly equal numbers of people.
examples: The IEBC announces new constituency boundaries just eight months before a general election. This violates the constitutional requirement that any review be completed at least 12 months before an election — the new boundaries cannot apply to the upcoming election.

A community notes that their constituency has three times the population of a neighbouring one, meaning their votes count for far less. The IEBC's boundary review must address this — the Constitution requires boundaries to reflect as equal a population quota as possible.

title: Allocation of party list seats
original_text: Elections for party list seats in Parliament and county assemblies shall be on the basis of proportional representation. Party lists must alternate between male and female candidates in priority order and reflect regional and ethnic diversity.
what_this_means: The special seats in Parliament (for youth, women, persons with disabilities) are allocated to parties based on how many seats they won in the general election. Parties must alternate men and women on their lists — not put all women at the bottom.
examples: A political party submits a party list for nominated seats with 10 men listed first and 2 women at the very end, knowing they will only qualify for 5 seats. This violates the constitutional requirement for alternation between male and female candidates — the list must alternate properly.

A party that wins 30% of elected seats in the National Assembly is entitled to 30% of the party list seats — not more, not less. The proportional representation principle ensures party list allocation matches actual voter support.

title: Basic requirements for political parties
original_text: Every political party shall have a national character, democratically elected governing body, promote national unity, practise democracy internally, respect the right to participate in the political process, promote human rights and gender equality, and subscribe to a code of conduct. A party shall not be founded on religious, ethnic, gender or racial basis, or encourage violence, maintain a militia, engage in corruption, or misuse public resources.
what_this_means: Political parties cannot be tribal, religious, or regional clubs. They must be open to all Kenyans, hold internal elections, respect human rights, and refuse to use violence or corruption. A party built around one ethnic group or religion is constitutionally invalid.
examples: A group tries to register a political party openly named after a specific ethnic community, with membership restricted to people from that community. This violates the constitutional requirement that parties have a national character — the Registrar of Political Parties must reject it.
A political party in a county covertly pays youth to disrupt an opponent's rallies. Encouraging violence is a constitutional prohibition — the party can be deregistered for this conduct.

title: Legislation on political parties
original_text: Parliament shall enact legislation to provide for equitable allocation of airtime to political parties, regulation of broadcasting during elections, regulation and supervision of political parties, establishment of a political parties fund, accounts and audit of parties, and restrictions on use of public resources for party promotion.
what_this_means: Parliament must pass laws ensuring political parties are properly regulated, funded fairly, and prevented from misusing government resources. State media must give all parties fair airtime — not just the ruling party.
examples: The national broadcaster gives the ruling party ten times more airtime than opposition parties during the election campaign period. The Constitution requires equitable airtime allocation — opposition parties can legally demand equal access to State media.

A Cabinet Secretary is found using a government vehicle and ministry staff to campaign for his party. The Constitution requires Parliament to restrict use of public resources for party promotion — this conduct is illegal and grounds for disciplinary action.

title: Establishment of Parliament
original_text: There is established a Parliament of Kenya, which shall consist of the National Assembly and the Senate. The National Assembly and the Senate shall perform their respective functions in accordance with this Constitution.
what_this_means: Kenya's Parliament is made up of two Houses — the National Assembly (for the people) and the Senate (for the counties). Each has its own role, and neither can completely replace the other.
examples: The ruling party passes a bill that affects county revenues through the National Assembly alone, bypassing the Senate. This is unconstitutional — bills affecting counties must go through both Houses, and the Senate exists specifically to protect county interests.

An MP claims the National Assembly has the power to overrule the Senate on all matters. This is wrong — both Houses have defined constitutional roles, and on matters concerning counties, the Senate has real power that the National Assembly cannot simply override.

title: Role of Parliament
original_text: The legislative authority of the Republic is derived from the people and vested in Parliament. Parliament manifests the diversity of the nation, represents the will of the people, and exercises their sovereignty. Parliament may also amend the Constitution and alter county boundaries as provided for.
what_this_means: Only Parliament can make laws for Kenya — no other body has that power unless Parliament specifically delegates it. Parliament does not make laws based on its own wishes — it represents and acts on behalf of the Kenyan people.
examples: The President issues an executive order that creates new taxes without Parliamentary approval. Only Parliament has the power to impose taxes — executive orders cannot create new financial obligations on citizens without legislative authority.

A government agency starts issuing regulations with the force of law without any Act of Parliament giving them that authority. This violates Article 94 — only Parliament can grant law-making power to other bodies, and it must be done explicitly.

title: Role of the National Assembly
original_text: The National Assembly represents the people of the constituencies. It deliberates on and resolves issues of concern to the people, enacts legislation, determines allocation of national revenue, appropriates funds for national government, exercises oversight over national revenue, reviews the conduct of the President and Deputy President, and approves declarations of war.
what_this_means: Your MP in the National Assembly is there to speak for you, pass laws, control how government money is spent, and hold the President accountable. They can even approve or reject declarations of war — it is not a role to be taken lightly.
examples: The national government announces it is spending billions on a project that was not in the approved budget. The National Assembly has the constitutional power to demand accountability — this is their oversight role and they can call ministers to explain.

Citizens in a constituency feel their MP never raises their concerns in Parliament, never votes on legislation, and is never in Nairobi. The MP is constitutionally required to deliberate on and resolve issues of concern to the people they represent — the electorate can recall an MP who fails in this duty.

title: Role of the Senate
original_text: The Senate represents the counties and serves to protect the interests of the counties and their governments. The Senate participates in law-making on Bills concerning counties, determines allocation of national revenue among counties, and participates in oversight of State officers including removal of the President.
what_this_means: Every county has senators in Nairobi specifically to defend county interests. If national legislation threatens county powers or money, the Senate must approve it. The Senate also plays a role in determining how much money each county gets.
examples: The national government passes a law reducing county revenue allocation below 15% of national revenue without Senate involvement. The Senate is constitutionally required to be involved in such decisions — the law is invalid without Senate participation.

The National Assembly votes to impeach the President. The Senate must then hold its own independent hearing — the President can only be removed if two-thirds of all senators vote to uphold the charges, giving the Senate a crucial check on presidential removal.

title: Membership of the National Assembly
original_text: The National Assembly consists of: 290 members elected from single-member constituencies; 47 women each elected from one county; 12 nominated members representing special interests including youth, persons with disabilities and workers; and the Speaker as an ex officio member.
what_this_means: The National Assembly has 349 members. Your direct representatives are the constituency MP you elect. On top of that, each county elects a woman MP, and parties nominate members to represent youth, disabled people, and workers — ensuring voices that might otherwise be left out are present.
examples: A voter in Kisumu county does not realise she can vote for both her constituency MP and a county woman representative. These are two separate votes for two different seats in the National Assembly — both are constitutionally provided for.

A party nominates only middle-aged men to the 12 nominated seats meant to represent youth, persons with disabilities, and workers. The Constitution specifies these seats are for those special interests — nominating people who don't represent them defeats the constitutional purpose.

title: Membership of the Senate
original_text: The Senate consists of: 47 members elected by counties; 16 women nominated by political parties proportionally; 2 youth members (one man, one woman); 2 members with disabilities (one man, one woman); and the Speaker as ex officio member.
what_this_means: The Senate has 67 members. Every county elects one senator directly. Beyond that, parties nominate women senators, youth representatives, and senators with disabilities — ensuring the Senate reflects the diversity of Kenyan society.
examples: Residents of Narok county go to vote for their Senator alongside other county elections. Each county is one constituency for Senate purposes — Narok's residents elect one Senator to represent their county's interests in the upper house.

A political party that wins 40% of the Senate elected seats gets 40% of the 16 nominated women's seats. The allocation is proportional to actual elected performance — parties cannot claim more nominated seats than their proportional share.

title: Qualifications and disqualifications for election as member of Parliament
original_text: A person is eligible for election as an MP if registered as a voter, meets educational and ethical requirements, and is nominated by a party or is an independent candidate supported by at least 1000 voters (National Assembly) or 2000 voters (Senate). Disqualifications include: being a public officer, holding IEBC office within 5 years, not being a Kenyan citizen for at least 10 years, being a county assembly member, being of unsound mind, being bankrupt, serving imprisonment of at least 6 months, or having abused public office.
what_this_means: You can run for Parliament if you are a registered voter, meet the educational and integrity requirements, and get enough voter signatures to support your nomination. But several things will disqualify you — including currently holding a public service job, being bankrupt, or serving a prison sentence.
examples: A civil servant working in the Kenya Revenue Authority wants to contest a parliamentary seat in the upcoming election. He must resign from the KRA before contesting — sitting public officers are constitutionally disqualified from running for Parliament.

A businesswoman who was declared bankrupt two years ago and whose case is still unresolved wants to run for a National Assembly seat. She is disqualified — undischarged bankrupts cannot stand for Parliament under the Constitution.

title: Promotion of representation of marginalised groups
original_text: Parliament shall enact legislation to promote the representation in Parliament of women, persons with disabilities, youth, ethnic and other minorities, and marginalised communities.
what_this_means: Parliament must actively legislate to ensure that women, young people, disabled persons, and minority and marginalised communities have real representation in Parliament — not just on paper, but in practice.
examples: Parliament passes a bill increasing the number of special seats for persons with disabilities in both houses. This is fulfilling the constitutional mandate to promote representation — the legislation is required, not optional.

Civil society organisations petition Parliament to enact the legislation required under this article after years of inaction. A court can order Parliament to fulfil this constitutional duty — failure to legislate can be legally challenged.

title: Election of members of Parliament
original_text: A general election of members of Parliament shall be held on the second Tuesday in August in every fifth year. Vacancies shall be filled through by-elections within 90 days for directly elected seats, and within 21 days for nominated seats. No vacancy shall be filled within 3 months immediately before a general election.
what_this_means: Parliamentary elections happen every five years on a set date — the second Tuesday in August. If an MP dies or vacates their seat, a by-election must be held quickly to fill the gap. But no by-elections happen in the three months just before a general election.
examples: An MP dies 10 months before the general election. A by-election must be held within 90 days to fill the seat — the constituency cannot go unrepresented for the remaining period before the general election.

An MP dies just two months before the scheduled general election. In this case, no by-election will be held — the vacancy falls within the three-month window before the general election, so the seat remains empty until the general election.

title: Term of Parliament
original_text: The term of each House of Parliament expires on the date of the next general election. When Kenya is at war, Parliament may, by resolution supported by at least two-thirds of each House, extend the term by not more than 6 months at a time, for a total of not more than 12 months.
what_this_means: Parliament lasts for five years. It can only be extended — and only during wartime — by a two-thirds majority of both Houses, and even then, for no more than 12 months in total. A President cannot extend Parliament unilaterally.
examples: A President announces that due to a security crisis, he is extending Parliament's term by 18 months without seeking Parliament's approval. This is unconstitutional — only Parliament itself can vote to extend its term, only during actual war, and by a two-thirds majority.

Kenya is formally at war with a neighbouring state, and Parliament votes to extend its own term by 6 months so the country does not hold an election during active conflict. This is constitutionally permitted — provided the two-thirds majority threshold is met in both Houses.

title: Vacation of office of member of Parliament
original_text: An MP's seat becomes vacant if they die, are absent for 8 sittings without permission, are removed under the Constitution, resign, defect from the party they were elected on (or join a party if elected as independent), or at the end of Parliament's term or become disqualified.
what_this_means: An MP can lose their seat for multiple reasons — including switching parties. If you were elected on a party ticket and you leave that party, your seat goes with it. If you ran as an independent and then join a party, you also lose your seat.
examples: An MP elected on the ODM ticket publicly resigns from ODM and declares support for a rival party. Under the Constitution, leaving the party you were elected on means you lose your parliamentary seat — the vacancy must be filled.

An MP is absent from Parliament for 10 consecutive sittings without seeking the Speaker's written permission and provides no acceptable explanation. The seat is declared vacant — MPs have a constitutional duty to actually attend and participate in Parliament.

title: Right of recall
original_text: The electorate under Articles 97 and 98 have the right to recall the member of Parliament representing their constituency before the end of the term of the relevant House of Parliament. Parliament shall enact legislation to provide for the grounds and procedure for recall.
what_this_means: Voters have the constitutional right to remove an MP before their term ends, if the MP fails them badly enough. Parliament must pass a law spelling out the grounds and procedure for doing this — but the right itself is in the Constitution.
examples: Residents of a constituency are furious that their MP has not visited the constituency once in three years, never raises local issues in Parliament, and is embroiled in corruption. They have a constitutional right to initiate a recall process — provided Parliament has enacted the required legislation specifying how.

An MP who is recalled before completing their term is not entitled to their full benefits as if they served the complete term. The recall is the people's mechanism to hold lazy or corrupt MPs accountable between elections.

title: Determination of questions of membership
original_text: The High Court shall hear and determine any question whether a person has been validly elected as a member of Parliament or whether the seat of a member has become vacant. Such questions shall be heard and determined within six months of the petition being lodged.
what_this_means: If you believe an election was rigged or that an MP has lost their seat, you take it to the High Court — and the court must hear and decide your case within six months. Justice delayed in election matters is justice denied.
examples: An election petition challenging the National Assembly results in a constituency is filed in the High Court. By law, the court must determine the case within six months — it cannot sit on the petition indefinitely while the potentially wrongly elected MP continues to serve.

A political party argues that one of their MPs automatically lost his seat when he was convicted of a crime, but the MP refuses to vacate. The High Court is the constitutionally designated body to make the final determination on whether the seat has become vacant.

title: Speakers and Deputy Speakers of Parliament
original_text: There shall be a Speaker for each House of Parliament, elected by that House from among persons qualified to be elected as MPs but who are not sitting members. There shall also be a Deputy Speaker elected from among the sitting members of each House.
what_this_means: Each House of Parliament has a Speaker who chairs proceedings and a Deputy Speaker who fills in when needed. The Speaker must be someone qualified to be an MP but cannot be a current sitting member — they must be an independent presiding officer.
examples: A sitting MP is nominated to become Speaker of the National Assembly. She must first vacate her constituency seat before assuming the role — the Constitution requires the Speaker to be someone qualified to be an MP but not a current sitting member.

The Speaker of the Senate is absent during an important vote. The Deputy Speaker, who is a sitting Senator, presides over the session — this is exactly the constitutional role the Deputy Speaker exists to fill.

title: Presiding in Parliament
original_text: At any sitting of a House of Parliament, the Speaker presides; in the absence of the Speaker, the Deputy Speaker presides; and in the absence of both, another member elected by the House presides. At a joint sitting of the two Houses, the Speaker of the National Assembly shall preside.
what_this_means: Parliamentary sessions always have a presiding officer — the Speaker, then the Deputy Speaker, then a member chosen by the House. At joint sittings of both Houses, the National Assembly Speaker is in charge.
examples: Both the Speaker and Deputy Speaker of the Senate are absent during an emergency session called to debate a crisis. The Senate must elect one of its sitting members to preside for that session — proceedings cannot go ahead without a presiding officer.
Parliament holds a joint sitting to hear the President's State of the Nation address. The Speaker of the National Assembly chairs the joint session — the Senate Speaker assists but does not lead.

title: Party leaders
original_text: There shall be a leader of the majority party and a leader of the minority party in the National Assembly. The majority party leader leads the largest party or coalition; the minority party leader leads the second largest. The order of precedence is: Speaker, majority leader, minority leader.
what_this_means: The National Assembly has a formal power structure — the Speaker presides over everyone, then the majority party leader who leads the ruling side, then the opposition leader. This ensures a structured check between government and opposition.
examples: After a coalition reshuffles its numbers in the National Assembly, a previously minority party now commands more seats than the ruling coalition. The leader of that party should become the new majority leader — the constitutional title follows actual numbers, not political convenience.

The government attempts to bypass the minority leader when scheduling debates on important legislation. The minority leader's role is constitutionally recognised — they have specific procedural rights and cannot simply be ignored in parliamentary proceedings.

title: Exercise of legislative powers
original_text: Parliament shall exercise its legislative power through Bills passed by Parliament and assented to by the President. Any Bill may originate in the National Assembly. Bills not concerning county government are considered only in the National Assembly. Bills concerning county government may originate in either House.
what_this_means: Laws are made through a clear process: Parliament passes a bill, and the President gives it assent. Most bills only go through the National Assembly. Bills that affect county governments must go through both Houses.
examples: Parliament passes a bill on immigration — a national matter with no county government implications. It only needs to go through the National Assembly and receive presidential assent — the Senate is not involved in this type of legislation.

A bill setting out how county revenue funds shall be managed is introduced in the National Assembly. Because it concerns county governments, it must also pass through the Senate before it can go to the President for assent.

title: Bills concerning county government
original_text: A Bill concerning county government includes bills affecting the functions and powers of county governments, bills relating to election of county assembly members or county executives, and certain financial bills affecting county governments. Such bills are either special bills or ordinary bills.
what_this_means: Not all bills go through both Houses — but if a bill touches county government powers, elections, or finances, it is a 'county bill' and must be dealt with by both the National Assembly and the Senate following specific procedures.
examples: The national government introduces a bill that changes how county governors are elected. This directly relates to county executive elections — it is a special bill that must follow the specific bicameral procedure for county-related legislation.

A bill is introduced in Parliament and there is a dispute about whether it affects county governments. The Speakers of both Houses must jointly resolve the question before the bill proceeds — this prevents one House from unilaterally classifying bills to their advantage.

title: Special Bills concerning county governments
original_text: Special Bills concerning county governments proceed like ordinary county Bills, but the National Assembly may amend or veto a Senate-passed special Bill only by a resolution supported by at least two-thirds of the National Assembly members.
what_this_means: For the most important county-related bills, the National Assembly cannot simply override the Senate by a simple majority — they need two-thirds of all members. This gives the Senate stronger protection over matters that directly affect county government structures.
examples: The Senate passes a special bill on county assembly elections. The National Assembly disagrees with some provisions and wants to amend it. They must get at least two-thirds of all 350 Assembly members to vote for the amendment — a simple majority is not enough.

The National Assembly tries to veto a special county bill by a vote of 180 to 170. The veto fails — two-thirds of all members (at least 233 votes) are required to veto a special county bill passed by the Senate.

title: Ordinary Bills concerning county governments
original_text: If one House passes an ordinary Bill concerning counties and the second House rejects it, it is referred to a mediation committee. If the second House passes it with amendments, it goes back to the originating House for reconsideration.
what_this_means: When the two Houses of Parliament disagree on a bill affecting counties, they must try to resolve their differences through a joint mediation committee — not just let one House steamroll the other.
examples: The National Assembly passes a bill on county land management. The Senate passes it with significant amendments. The bill goes back to the National Assembly — which can accept the Senate's version, or if they reject it, both Houses must go to a mediation committee to agree on a single version.

After mediation fails to produce an agreed bill, the bill is defeated entirely. This is the constitutional outcome — if both Houses cannot agree through mediation, the bill dies and must be reintroduced from scratch.

title: Mediation committees
original_text: If a Bill is referred to a mediation committee, the Speakers of both Houses shall appoint a committee with equal numbers from each House to develop a version both Houses will pass. If mediation fails within 30 days or either House rejects the mediated version, the Bill is defeated.
what_this_means: When Parliament's two Houses cannot agree on a bill, equal numbers of MPs and Senators sit together to find a compromise. If they cannot agree within 30 days, or if either House rejects the compromise, the bill dies completely — there is no way to force it through.
examples: A county finance bill is stuck between the two Houses with fundamentally different positions. A mediation committee of five MPs and five Senators works for 28 days to produce a compromise version. Both Houses must then vote to approve it — if either votes it down, the bill is finished.

A mediation committee cannot reach agreement within the 30-day deadline set by the Constitution. The bill automatically dies. The government must start the entire legislative process again if they still want the law — there are no shortcuts.

title: Money Bills
original_text: A money Bill may only deal with matters relating to taxes, charges on public funds, appropriation of public money, raising or guaranteeing loans, or incidental matters. A money Bill may only be introduced in the National Assembly.
what_this_means: Bills about taxing people or spending public money can only start in the National Assembly — not in the Senate. This ensures that decisions about money ultimately come from the House directly elected by all Kenyans.
examples: A Senator introduces a bill proposing a new levy on goods imported into Kenya. This is a money bill — it cannot start in the Senate and must be reintroduced in the National Assembly.

The National Assembly proposes the annual Appropriation Bill — the law that authorises government spending for the year. This is a money Bill and correctly originates in the National Assembly. It cannot originate in the Senate.

title: Presidential assent and referral
original_text: Within 14 days after receipt of a Bill, the President shall assent or refer it back to Parliament with reservations. Parliament may amend the Bill accommodating reservations or pass it unchanged by two-thirds of both Houses. If the President does not act within the specified period, the Bill is taken to have been assented to.
what_this_means: The President has 14 days to sign a bill into law or send it back to Parliament with objections. Parliament can override presidential objections by a two-thirds majority. If the President does nothing within the deadline, the bill automatically becomes law.
examples: Parliament passes a healthcare bill and sends it to the President. The President sits on it for 20 days without acting. The bill automatically becomes law on day 14 — the President cannot kill a bill through inaction.

The President sends a controversial bill back to Parliament citing specific constitutional concerns. Parliament debates the concerns but votes to pass the bill unchanged with a two-thirds majority of both Houses. The President must now assent within 7 days — they cannot veto it a second time.

title: Coming into force of laws
original_text: A Bill passed by Parliament and assented to by the President shall be published in the Gazette as an Act of Parliament within 7 days. An Act of Parliament comes into force on the 14th day after publication unless the Act specifies a different date. Acts that confer financial benefit on MPs shall not come into force until after the next general election.
what_this_means: Once a bill is signed, it must be published in the Gazette within 7 days and becomes law 14 days later — unless the law itself specifies a different start date. MPs cannot pass laws that immediately give themselves a financial benefit — those must wait until after the next election.
examples: Parliament passes a law raising MPs' salaries significantly. Even after presidential assent, this law cannot come into effect immediately — because it confers a financial benefit on MPs, it cannot take effect until after the next general election.
A new food safety law is published in the Gazette on 1st March. Unless the law specifies otherwise, it comes into force on 15th March — businesses must be compliant from that date.

title: Powers, privileges and immunities
original_text: There shall be freedom of speech and debate in Parliament. Parliament may provide for the powers, privileges and immunities of Parliament, its committees, and members.
what_this_means: What an MP says in Parliament — including criticisms and accusations — cannot be used against them in a court of law. This freedom of speech in Parliament exists to allow MPs to debate freely without fear of lawsuits for their contributions in the chamber.
examples: An MP makes a speech in Parliament accusing a minister of corruption, naming specific companies. The minister cannot sue the MP for defamation based on statements made in Parliament — parliamentary proceedings enjoy constitutional immunity.

An MP tries to hold a parliamentary committee hostage by threatening to leak information discussed in confidence. The committee's proceedings also enjoy certain constitutional protections — but this protection covers legitimate parliamentary business, not threats or abuse of the privilege.

title: Public access and participation
original_text: Parliament shall conduct its business in an open manner, and its sittings and committee sittings shall be in public. Parliament shall facilitate public participation and involvement in the legislative process. Parliament may only exclude the public in exceptional circumstances where the Speaker determines justifiable reasons exist.
what_this_means: Parliament belongs to the people — its sessions are public by default. Citizens have the right to participate in lawmaking, not just watch. Parliament cannot routinely hold important business behind closed doors.
examples: A parliamentary committee meets in private to discuss a controversial bill on media regulation without public participation. This is unconstitutional — committee sittings must be public and must include opportunities for the public to contribute.

Residents of a county submit written memoranda to the Senate committee considering a healthcare bill that affects their county. The Constitution requires Parliament to facilitate public participation — these submissions must be considered, not ignored.

title: Right to petition Parliament
original_text: Every person has a right to petition Parliament to consider any matter within its authority, including to enact, amend or repeal any legislation. Parliament shall make provision for the procedure for the exercise of this right.
what_this_means: You have a constitutional right to formally ask Parliament to consider any matter — including demanding they pass a new law, change an existing one, or repeal a law that harms people. Parliament must have a clear process for receiving and considering these petitions.
examples: A disability rights organisation gathers 50,000 signatures and submits a petition to Parliament demanding the amendment of the traffic act to better protect pedestrians with disabilities. Parliament must receive and consider this petition — it is a constitutional right, not just a courtesy.

A single parent submits a petition to the National Assembly asking Parliament to review laws on child support to make enforcement faster and fairer. Parliament cannot simply ignore petitions — the Constitution requires a procedure for handling them and they must be considered.

title: Official languages of Parliament
original_text: The official languages of Parliament shall be Kiswahili, English and Kenyan Sign Language, and business of Parliament may be conducted in English, Kiswahili and Kenyan Sign Language. In case of conflict between language versions of an Act, the version signed by the President shall prevail.
what_this_means: Parliament conducts its business in Kiswahili, English, and Kenyan Sign Language — all three are official. This means Kenyan Sign Language has constitutional standing in Parliament, and laws must not create conflicts between different language versions.
examples: A Member of Parliament gives a speech in the National Assembly entirely in Kiswahili. This is fully constitutionally valid — Kiswahili is an official language of Parliament and MPs can use it freely.

Two versions of an Act of Parliament — one in English and one in Kiswahili — appear to have different meanings in a specific provision. The court looks at the version that was signed by the President to determine the official legal meaning.

title: Quorum
original_text: The quorum of Parliament shall be 50 members in the case of the National Assembly or 15 members in the case of the Senate.
what_this_means: The National Assembly cannot conduct official business unless at least 50 of its 350 members are present. The Senate needs at least 15 members present. Votes and decisions taken without a quorum are constitutionally invalid.
examples: A contentious vote is called in the National Assembly late at night when only 30 members are present. Because the quorum of 50 is not met, any vote taken is unconstitutional — opposition members can challenge the validity of the result.
A bill is hurriedly passed by the Senate with only 10 Senators present. The quorum required is 15 — the bill was passed without a constitutional quorum and the passage is legally invalid.

title: Voting in Parliament
original_text: Except as otherwise provided, questions in either House shall be determined by a majority of members present and voting. The Speaker has no vote. In the case of a tie, the question is lost. A member shall not vote on any question in which they have a pecuniary interest.
what_this_means: Parliament decides by majority of those who show up and vote — not majority of all members (unless the Constitution specifies otherwise for certain matters). The Speaker never votes. If it ends in a tie, the motion fails. MPs cannot vote on decisions where they personally stand to gain money.
examples: A bill passes with 175 MPs in favour and 175 against. Because it is a tie, the bill is defeated — the Speaker cannot cast a deciding vote, and there is no concept of the Speaker's vote as a tiebreaker in Parliament.

A procurement bill that would directly benefit a company an MP owns comes up for a vote. That MP is constitutionally prohibited from voting — voting on a question where you have a financial interest violates the Constitution.

title: Decisions of Senate
original_text: When the Senate votes on matters affecting counties, each county delegation votes as one unit, with the delegation head casting the single vote after consulting fellow delegation members. The matter carries only if supported by a majority of all county delegations.
what_this_means: In the Senate, when voting on matters that affect counties, it is not individual senators who vote — it is county delegations as a whole. Each county gets one vote, not one vote per senator. This ensures no big county dominates small ones in county-related decisions.
examples: The Senate is voting on the allocation of national revenue among counties — a matter that directly affects counties. Nairobi's delegation of multiple senators casts just one vote on behalf of Nairobi county, same as Lamu's smaller delegation. Every county gets an equal voice.

A majority of individual senators support a county-related bill, but only 18 out of 47 county delegations as units vote in favour. The bill fails — county-affecting decisions require a majority of delegations (24 or more), not a majority of individual senators.

title: Committees and Standing Orders
original_text: Each House of Parliament may establish committees and shall make Standing Orders for the orderly conduct of its proceedings. Parliament may establish joint committees. Proceedings of a House are not invalid merely because of a vacancy in membership or the presence of an unauthorised person.
what_this_means: Parliamentary committees do the detailed work of scrutinising bills, investigating government, and calling officials to account. Their work is legitimate even if there are vacancies in membership — Parliament doesn't have to be at full capacity to function.
examples: A Senate committee investigating county finance irregularities has three of its seven members absent due to illness. The committee can still legally proceed with its work — a vacancy in membership does not invalidate parliamentary committee proceedings.

The National Assembly establishes a Public Accounts Committee tasked with scrutinising government spending. Cabinet Secretaries must appear before it and answer questions — the committee has constitutional authority to conduct its oversight role.

title: Power to call for evidence
original_text: Either House of Parliament and any of its committees has power to summon any person to appear before it for the purpose of giving evidence or providing information. For this purpose, Parliament and its committees have the same powers as the High Court to enforce attendance of witnesses and compel production of documents.
what_this_means: Parliamentary committees are powerful — they can summon anyone, including ministers, CEOs, and officials, to come and answer questions. Just like a court, they can enforce attendance and demand documents. Refusing to appear when summoned can be treated like contempt of court.
examples: A parliamentary committee investigating a government scandal summons the CEO of a parastatal to testify. The CEO refuses to appear. The committee can treat this as contempt and use its High Court equivalent powers to enforce his attendance — he cannot simply ignore the summons.

A National Assembly committee investigating irregular procurement demands all contracts and financial records from a ministry. The ministry must produce those documents — Parliament has the same power as the High Court to compel document production.

title: Location of sittings of Parliament
original_text: A sitting of either House may be held at any place within Kenya and may commence at any time the House appoints. When a new House is elected, the President shall appoint the place and date for the first sitting, not more than 30 days after the election.
what_this_means: Parliament does not have to meet only in Nairobi. It can hold sessions anywhere in Kenya. After a general election, the President must summon the new Parliament within 30 days — there can be no prolonged delay in constituting a new Parliament.
examples: Parliament decides to hold a special session in Kisumu to hear public views on lake fishing legislation. This is constitutionally valid — Parliament can sit anywhere in Kenya, not just at Parliament Buildings in Nairobi.

After a general election, the President delays announcing the date for Parliament's first sitting for 40 days. This is unconstitutional — the first sitting must be scheduled within 30 days of the election.

title: Parliamentary Service Commission
original_text: There is established the Parliamentary Service Commission responsible for providing services and facilities to Parliament, constituting parliamentary offices, preparing estimates of parliamentary expenditure, and undertaking programmes to promote parliamentary democracy.
what_this_means: The Parliamentary Service Commission runs the day-to-day administration of Parliament — managing staff, facilities, and budgets. It keeps Parliament functioning as an institution independently of the executive government.
examples: The government attempts to cut the parliamentary budget through the Treasury in a way that limits Parliament's ability to function effectively. The Parliamentary Service Commission prepares Parliament's own budget estimates — Parliament's financial independence is part of the constitutional separation of powers.

Parliament needs to hire additional research staff for its committees. The Parliamentary Service Commission handles all parliamentary staffing — it does not need approval from the Public Service Commission, protecting parliamentary independence.

title: Clerks and staff of Parliament
original_text: There shall be a Clerk for each House of Parliament, appointed by the Parliamentary Service Commission with the approval of the relevant House. The offices of the Clerks and their staff are offices in the Parliamentary Service.
what_this_means: Each House has a Clerk who manages the administrative and procedural running of that House. The Clerk is accountable to Parliament through the Parliamentary Service Commission — not to any government ministry.
examples: The Cabinet Secretary for Public Service attempts to issue directives to the Clerks of Parliament regarding how they process bills. The Clerks are parliamentary service officials, not government employees — they do not fall under executive direction.

The Parliamentary Service Commission appoints a new Clerk of the National Assembly after the previous one retires. The National Assembly must also approve the appointment — this two-step process ensures the Clerk is both professionally qualified and has the confidence of the House.

title: Principles of executive authority
original_text: Executive authority derives from the people of Kenya and shall be exercised in accordance with this Constitution. Executive authority shall be exercised in a manner compatible with the principle of service to the people of Kenya, and for their well-being and benefit.
what_this_means: Government power comes from the people and must be used for the people. The executive — the President, Deputy President, and Cabinet — exists to serve Kenyans, not to enrich itself or exercise unchecked power.
examples: The President issues executive directives that benefit a small group of businesspeople at the expense of ordinary Kenyans. Executive authority must be exercised for the well-being of all Kenyans — selective use that harms the public interest can be challenged in court.

A Cabinet Secretary implements a policy without any public benefit but with significant personal gain for politically connected individuals. Since executive power must serve the people's well-being, such a policy can be challenged as an unconstitutional exercise of executive authority.

title: The National Executive
original_text: The national executive of the Republic comprises the President, the Deputy President and the rest of the Cabinet. The composition of the national executive shall reflect the regional and ethnic diversity of the people of Kenya.
what_this_means: Kenya's executive government is the President, Deputy President, and Cabinet combined — not the President alone. The Cabinet must reflect Kenya's ethnic and regional diversity, meaning the President cannot fill it with people from only one region or community.
examples: A President appoints a Cabinet in which 18 out of 22 Cabinet Secretaries come from one ethnic community. This violates the constitutional requirement that the national executive reflect the regional and ethnic diversity of Kenya — it can be challenged in court.

The Deputy President and President together form just the top layer of executive power. The Cabinet Secretaries are also constitutionally part of the national executive — decisions that affect the country cannot be made by the President and Deputy President alone, bypassing Cabinet.

title: Authority of the President
original_text: The President is the Head of State and Government, Commander-in-Chief of the Kenya Defence Forces, chairperson of the National Security Council, and a symbol of national unity. The President shall respect and uphold the Constitution, safeguard sovereignty, promote unity, promote respect for diversity, and ensure protection of human rights.
what_this_means: The President is Kenya's most powerful official — but the Constitution defines exactly what that power is and what duties come with it. The President must protect human rights, respect diversity, and uphold the Constitution — not just command and rule.
examples: The President publicly endorses discrimination against a specific religious minority. This directly contradicts the constitutional duty to promote respect for diversity and ensure protection of human rights — such conduct can be cited in impeachment proceedings.

The President orders the military to violently suppress a peaceful protest. As Commander-in-Chief, the President leads the military, but all military actions must comply with the Constitution and respect human rights — unlawful orders can be refused and challenged.

title: Functions of the President
original_text: The President shall address the opening of each new Parliament, address Parliament at least once yearly, report to the nation on national values progress, submit reports on international obligations, nominate and appoint key State officers with National Assembly approval, chair Cabinet meetings, and may declare a state of emergency or war with Parliament's approval.
what_this_means: The President has specific, defined functions — from appointing Cabinet Secretaries to declaring war. Most major appointments and decisions require Parliamentary approval. The President cannot act unilaterally on the most significant matters of state.
examples: The President appoints a new Attorney-General and instructs them to begin work without seeking National Assembly approval. The Constitution requires the National Assembly to approve this appointment — the appointment is not valid until Parliament approves it.
Kenya is attacked by a neighbouring country. The President wants to declare war but cannot do so alone — the Constitution requires Parliament's approval before war can be officially declared.

title: Power of mercy
original_text: On petition, the President may grant a free or conditional pardon, postpone or substitute punishment, or remit all or part of a punishment — but only in accordance with the advice of an Advisory Committee comprising the Attorney-General, the Cabinet Secretary for correctional services, and at least five others appointed by Parliament.
what_this_means: The President can pardon convicted criminals — but not on their own initiative. A specific advisory committee must recommend the pardon, and the President acts on their advice. This prevents pardons being used as political rewards.
examples: A prisoner serving a long sentence for a crime she committed when she was 17 petitions for a pardon based on her rehabilitation and good conduct in prison. The Advisory Committee reviews her case and may recommend a pardon — the President can then grant it based on that advice.

The President announces a personal decision to pardon a political ally convicted of corruption, without going through the Advisory Committee. This is unconstitutional — the power of mercy must be exercised on the advice of the committee, not as a personal presidential favour.

title: Exercise of presidential powers during temporary incumbency
original_text: A person holding office as President during an election period or while the President is incapacitated may not make new judicial appointments, appoint or dismiss Cabinet Secretaries or ambassadors, exercise the power of mercy, or confer honours.
what_this_means: When the President is temporarily absent, incapacitated, or there is a transitional period during elections, whoever is holding the presidency cannot make major permanent decisions. This prevents last-minute stacking of courts or rewarding of allies before a new President takes over.
examples: Kenya's President is in hospital and the Deputy President is acting President. During that period, the acting President cannot make new judicial appointments — such decisions must wait for the President's return or for a permanent new President to take office.

During the gap between an election result and the swearing-in of the new President, the outgoing President tries to appoint five new High Court judges. This is prohibited — the Constitution specifically bars such appointments during election transition periods.

title: Decisions of the President
original_text: A decision of the President in the performance of any function under this Constitution shall be in writing and shall bear the seal and signature of the President.
what_this_means: Every official decision the President makes must be in writing with their signature and seal. Verbal presidential orders or undocumented decisions do not have constitutional force.
examples: A ministry begins implementing a policy claiming the President verbally approved it in a meeting. Without a written, signed and sealed presidential decision, that approval has no constitutional force — it is not an official presidential decision.
The President signs a written decree appointing a new Principal Secretary, with the official State seal affixed. This meets the constitutional requirement — the appointment is formally valid.

title: Election of the President
original_text: The President shall be elected by registered voters in a national election. Presidential elections shall be held on the second Tuesday in August in every fifth year, or in circumstances of vacancy.
what_this_means: The President is chosen directly by all Kenyan voters — not by Parliament or any other body. Presidential elections happen on the same day as other general elections, every five years on the second Tuesday of August.
examples: A group proposes that future Presidents should be elected by Parliament rather than by direct popular vote, to save money. This would require a constitutional amendment — the direct election of the President by Kenyan citizens is a fundamental constitutional provision.

The general election is held on the second Tuesday of August. All presidential, parliamentary, county governor, and county assembly elections happen on the same day. Voting for President is a direct national vote by every registered Kenyan.

title: Qualifications and disqualifications for election as President
original_text: A presidential candidate must be a citizen by birth, eligible to stand for Parliament, nominated by a political party or as an independent candidate, and nominated by at least 2000 voters from each of a majority of counties. Disqualifications include owing allegiance to a foreign state or holding a public office.
what_this_means: To run for President, you must be born Kenyan, meet MP eligibility standards, and demonstrate broad national support by collecting 2000 voter signatures from more than half of Kenya's 47 counties — proving your support is truly national, not just regional.
examples: A naturalised Kenyan citizen — born in Uganda but granted Kenyan citizenship — wants to run for President. He cannot — the Constitution requires presidential candidates to be citizens by birth, not by registration.

A politician wants to run for President but is still serving as a Principal Secretary. She must resign from her public office before contesting — active public officers are disqualified from standing for election as President.

title: Procedure at presidential election
original_text: A presidential candidate must receive more than half of all votes cast AND at least 25% of votes cast in more than half of all counties to be declared elected. If no candidate meets this threshold, a fresh election is held within 30 days between the top two candidates.
what_this_means: Winning the presidency requires a double threshold — more than 50% of total votes, AND support spread across at least 24 of Kenya's 47 counties. A candidate who wins 60% of votes but only from two or three counties does not become President — national spread matters.
examples: Candidate A gets 51% of all votes cast but achieves 25% of the vote in only 20 counties. She has not met the constitutional threshold — she needs to hit 25% in at least 24 counties. A runoff election must be held between the top two candidates.
Only one candidate is formally nominated before the nomination deadline — no other candidate files. The sole candidate is automatically declared elected as President without a vote being held.

title: Death before assuming office
original_text: If a President-elect dies after being declared elected but before assuming office, the Deputy President-elect shall be sworn in as acting President, and a fresh presidential election shall be held within 60 days. If both President-elect and Deputy President-elect die, the Speaker of the National Assembly shall act as President.
what_this_means: The Constitution has a clear succession plan if a tragedy occurs before inauguration. The Deputy President-elect steps up immediately as acting President and Kenya holds a new election within 60 days to choose a new President.
examples: The declared President-elect is killed in an accident before the inauguration date. The Deputy President-elect is immediately sworn in as acting President, and Kenya must hold a fresh presidential election within 60 days.

In a worst-case scenario where both the President-elect and Deputy President-elect die before inauguration, the Speaker of the National Assembly serves as acting President until a new election produces a winner.

title: Questions as to validity of presidential election
original_text: A person may file a petition in the Supreme Court to challenge the election of the President-elect within 7 days after the declaration of results. The Supreme Court shall hear and determine the petition within 14 days and its decision shall be final. If the election is invalid, a fresh election shall be held within 60 days.
what_this_means: If you believe the presidential election was rigged, you have exactly 7 days to file a petition with the Supreme Court. The court then has 14 days to decide — no extensions. The Supreme Court's decision is final and cannot be appealed anywhere.
examples: A presidential candidate believes the IEBC manipulated results and wants to challenge the election. He must file his petition at the Supreme Court within 7 days of the result declaration — waiting even one day beyond that window means his case cannot be heard.

The Supreme Court hears a presidential election petition and rules the election invalid. The IEBC must organise a fresh presidential election within 60 days of that ruling — there is no way to delay or avoid a new election once the court rules the original one invalid.

title: Assumption of office of President
original_text: The swearing in of the President-elect shall be in public before the Chief Justice (or Deputy Chief Justice if unavailable). The President-elect shall be sworn in on the first Tuesday following the 14th day after results are declared (if no petition), or the 7th day after the Supreme Court confirms the election is valid.
what_this_means: The inauguration of a new President happens on a specific day set by the Constitution, in public, before the Chief Justice. There is no room for a President to delay their own swearing-in, or to swear in a President in secret.
examples: Presidential results are declared on 15th August with no petition filed. The President-elect must be sworn in on the first Tuesday after 29th August — the date is constitutionally fixed, not discretionary.

A President-elect tries to hold a private swearing-in ceremony before the constitutionally set date. This is invalid — the oath must be taken in public, before the Chief Justice, on the constitutionally prescribed date.

title: Term of office of President
original_text: The President shall hold office for a term beginning on the date of being sworn in, and ending when the next elected President is sworn in. A person shall not hold office as President for more than two terms.
what_this_means: The President serves a five-year term and can only serve two terms total — whether consecutive or not. No President can change the Constitution to give themselves a third term, except through the full constitutional amendment process including a referendum.
examples: A President who served two full terms steps down and then ten years later wants to run again. The two-term limit under this article is absolute — regardless of how many years have passed, a person who has served two terms cannot hold the presidency again.

A sitting President promotes the idea of staying in power through a constitutional amendment that removes term limits. Such an amendment would require a national referendum — and even the attempt can be resisted as an affront to the Constitution's design.

title: Protection from legal proceedings
original_text: Criminal proceedings shall not be instituted or continued in any court against the President during their tenure. Civil proceedings shall not be brought against the President during tenure for anything done in the exercise of presidential powers under the Constitution. This immunity does not extend to crimes under international law.
what_this_means: A sitting President cannot be taken to a Kenyan court — but this immunity expires the moment they leave office. And it does not protect them from international criminal charges — if charged with crimes against humanity or war crimes, the immunity does not apply.
examples: A businessman wants to sue the President for breach of contract over a deal made before the President took office. He must wait — civil proceedings against the sitting President for personal matters cannot proceed during the presidential term.
