The International Criminal Court issues charges against a sitting Kenyan President for crimes against humanity. The presidential immunity does not extend to international criminal proceedings — the President cannot use it to block ICC prosecution.

title: Removal of President on grounds of incapacity
original_text: A quarter of all National Assembly members may move a motion to investigate the President's mental or physical incapacity. If supported by a majority of all National Assembly members, the Chief Justice appoints a medical tribunal within 7 days. If the tribunal finds the President incapable, and two-thirds of the National Assembly ratify, the President ceases to hold office.
what_this_means: If a President is too ill to perform their duties, Parliament — through a medical process — can remove them. This is a health-based removal, not a punishment. It requires a very clear medical determination by qualified professionals.
examples: The President suffers a serious stroke that leaves them unable to communicate or make decisions, but the Deputy President refuses to take over. MPs can initiate this process — a quarter of all members start the motion and the rest of the constitutional process follows to ensure Kenya has a functioning head of state.

The medical tribunal appointed by the Chief Justice determines that the President is fully capable of performing their duties. The National Assembly must accept this finding — the process is designed around medical expertise, not political judgment.

title: Removal of President by impeachment
original_text: A third of all National Assembly members may move an impeachment motion on grounds of gross constitutional violation, commission of a crime under national or international law, or gross misconduct. If two-thirds of the Assembly support the motion, the Senate investigates and may vote to remove the President by a two-thirds majority of all senators.
what_this_means: Impeachment is the political process for removing a President who has seriously failed their constitutional duties. It requires a very high threshold — two-thirds of the National Assembly must first pass the impeachment, and then two-thirds of the Senate must vote to uphold it.
examples: A President is caught on camera directly ordering police to shoot unarmed civilians. MPs present the evidence to the National Assembly — if a third support starting the process and two-thirds ultimately vote in favour, the case goes to the Senate for a full hearing and final determination.

The Senate investigates an impeachment charge but only 60% of senators vote to remove the President. Because the Constitution requires two-thirds of all senators, the President survives impeachment and returns to full duties — the high threshold is deliberately set to prevent political abuse of the process.

title: Vacancy in the office of President
original_text: The office of President becomes vacant on death, resignation, or removal. On vacancy, the Deputy President assumes the presidency for the remainder of the term. If the Deputy President cannot fill the vacancy, the Speaker of the National Assembly acts as President and a fresh election must be held within 60 days.
what_this_means: If the President's office falls vacant, there is a clear and immediate succession plan. The Deputy President takes over — and if they cannot, the Speaker steps in temporarily while a new election is prepared. Kenya is never without a head of state.
examples: The President resigns from office by delivering a written resignation to the Speaker of the National Assembly. The Deputy President is immediately and automatically elevated to President for the remainder of the term — no election is needed unless the term had less than two and a half years remaining.

Both the President and Deputy President are removed from office simultaneously by impeachment. The Speaker of the National Assembly becomes acting President and a fresh presidential election must be held within 60 days.

title: Functions of the Deputy President
original_text: The Deputy President shall be the principal assistant of the President and shall deputise for the President in the execution of the President's functions. The Deputy President performs functions assigned by the President. When the President is absent or temporarily incapacitated, the Deputy President shall act as the President.
what_this_means: The Deputy President is the President's number two — not just a title holder. When the President is away or unable to act, the Deputy President steps in fully with all presidential authority. The President can also assign the Deputy President specific functions.
examples: The President travels abroad for an international summit. The Deputy President automatically acts as President during that period — they can sign documents, issue directives, and carry out presidential functions with full authority.

A President tries to maintain all presidential powers personally even while medically incapacitated in hospital, refusing to let the Deputy President act. The Constitution is clear — when the President is temporarily incapacitated, the Deputy President shall act as President.

title: Election and swearing-in of Deputy President
original_text: Each presidential candidate shall nominate a person as a running mate for Deputy President. The IEBC shall declare the running mate of the elected President as the elected Deputy President. The Deputy President is sworn in alongside the President and serves a maximum of two terms.
what_this_means: The Deputy President is not independently elected — they are the running mate chosen by the presidential candidate. When you vote for a presidential candidate, you are automatically also voting for their Deputy President pick.
examples: A voter assumes she can vote for one presidential candidate while voting for a different person's running mate as Deputy President. This is not how the Constitution works — the Deputy President is the running mate of whoever wins the presidency; they come as a pair.

A Deputy President who has served two full terms as Deputy President cannot serve as Deputy President again — the two-term limit on the deputy presidency is constitutionally enshrined just as it is for the presidency.

title: Vacancy in the office of Deputy President
original_text: Within 14 days after a vacancy in the office of Deputy President arises, the President shall nominate a person to fill the vacancy, and the National Assembly shall vote on the nomination within 60 days.
what_this_means: If the Deputy Presidency falls vacant, the President must quickly nominate a replacement, and Parliament must confirm or reject the nominee within 60 days. The country cannot go without a Deputy President for an extended period.
examples: The Deputy President resigns to run for a different political position. The President has 14 days to nominate a new Deputy President. The National Assembly then has 60 days to vote on whether to approve the nomination.

The President takes three months to nominate a new Deputy President after the previous one died in office. This delay is unconstitutional — the nomination must happen within 14 days of the vacancy arising.

title: Removal of Deputy President
original_text: The Deputy President may be removed from office on grounds of physical or mental incapacity or by impeachment for gross constitutional violation, criminal conduct under national or international law, or gross misconduct. The same procedures that apply to removing the President apply to removing the Deputy President.
what_this_means: The Deputy President can be removed from office through the same processes as the President — either on health grounds through a medical tribunal or through impeachment by Parliament. The same high thresholds apply.
examples: The Deputy President is accused of orchestrating a bribery scheme. A third of National Assembly members can initiate impeachment proceedings using exactly the same process as for presidential impeachment — the constitutional protections and thresholds are identical.

The Deputy President develops a serious health condition that makes performing their functions impossible. The same medical tribunal process used for the President can be initiated to formally remove the Deputy President on grounds of incapacity.

title: Remuneration and benefits of President and Deputy President
original_text: The remuneration and benefits of the President and Deputy President shall be a charge on the Consolidated Fund. Their benefits cannot be reduced while in office. The retirement benefits of former Presidents and Deputy Presidents shall not be varied to their disadvantage during their lifetime.
what_this_means: The President and Deputy President's pay is guaranteed by the Constitution and paid directly from the national treasury — Parliament cannot cut their salaries to pressure them politically. Their retirement benefits are also protected for life after leaving office.
examples: Parliament, in a dispute with the President, votes to cut the presidential salary to zero as a pressure tactic. This is unconstitutional — the Constitution guarantees the President's remuneration and it cannot be varied to their disadvantage while in office.

A former President's retirement benefits are reduced by a subsequent administration unhappy with the previous President's record. This is unconstitutional — retirement benefits of former Presidents are protected and cannot be reduced during their lifetime.

title: Cabinet
original_text: The Cabinet consists of the President, the Deputy President, the Attorney-General, and not fewer than 14 and not more than 22 Cabinet Secretaries. Cabinet Secretaries are nominated by the President and appointed with National Assembly approval. A Cabinet Secretary shall not be a Member of Parliament.
what_this_means: The Cabinet has a minimum of 14 and maximum of 22 Cabinet Secretaries — limiting its size to prevent bloated government. Every Cabinet Secretary must be approved by the National Assembly, and they cannot also be a sitting MP — you cannot be a Cabinet Secretary and an MP at the same time.
examples: The President wants to nominate an MP she trusts as a Cabinet Secretary. The MP must first resign from Parliament — a Cabinet Secretary cannot simultaneously hold a seat in Parliament, and the Constitution is explicit about this.
The President nominates 25 Cabinet Secretaries. Even if all are well qualified, this exceeds the constitutional maximum of 22 — the nominations that push beyond the cap are constitutionally invalid.

title: Decisions, responsibility and accountability of the Cabinet
original_text: A decision by the Cabinet shall be in writing. Cabinet Secretaries are accountable individually and collectively to the President. A Cabinet Secretary shall attend before Parliament committees when required and answer questions about their docket. Cabinet Secretaries shall provide Parliament with full and regular reports.
what_this_means: Cabinet is collectively responsible for its decisions, and each Cabinet Secretary is personally accountable for their ministry. They cannot hide from Parliament — they must appear before parliamentary committees and answer questions about how they are running their ministries.
examples: The Health Cabinet Secretary refuses to appear before the National Assembly's Health Committee, claiming the session is not convenient. The Constitution requires Cabinet Secretaries to attend when required by Parliament — refusal is a constitutional violation.

A Cabinet Secretary claims in public that a decision was made personally by the President and the Cabinet cannot be blamed. The Constitution requires Cabinet decisions to be collective — the entire Cabinet is responsible for decisions made by the Cabinet collectively.

title: Secretary to the Cabinet
original_text: There is established the office of Secretary to the Cabinet. The Secretary is nominated and appointed by the President with National Assembly approval and may be dismissed by the President. The Secretary manages Cabinet business and keeps Cabinet minutes.
what_this_means: The Secretary to the Cabinet is the senior civil servant who runs the administrative machinery of Cabinet — managing meetings, keeping minutes, and conveying decisions. They are a public service position, not a political appointee in the partisan sense.
examples: The Secretary to the Cabinet secretly destroys Cabinet minutes from a controversial meeting. This is a serious breach — Cabinet decisions must be documented in writing and kept officially. Destroying records is unconstitutional and potentially criminal.

The President fires the Secretary to the Cabinet overnight without any stated reason. While the President can dismiss the Secretary, even dismissal from public office must follow due process principles — the Secretary can challenge an arbitrary dismissal.

title: Principal Secretaries
original_text: There is established the office of Principal Secretary. Each State department is under the administration of a Principal Secretary. The President nominates from persons recommended by the Public Service Commission and appoints with National Assembly approval.
what_this_means: Principal Secretaries are the top civil servants running individual government departments. Their appointment goes through the Public Service Commission and Parliament — they are not purely presidential personal picks.
examples: The President wants to appoint a personal ally as Principal Secretary in the Treasury without going through the Public Service Commission. This is unconstitutional — Principal Secretary nominees must come from persons recommended by the Public Service Commission.

The National Assembly rejects a President's nominated Principal Secretary during the vetting process. The nomination fails — National Assembly approval is required, and Parliament's rejection means the person cannot be appointed.

title: Attorney-General
original_text: There is established the office of Attorney-General. The Attorney-General is the principal legal adviser to the government, represents the national government in court, and shall promote, protect and uphold the rule of law and defend the public interest. The Attorney-General is appointed by the President with National Assembly approval.
what_this_means: The Attorney-General is Kenya's top government lawyer — but their primary duty is to the law and public interest, not personal loyalty to the President. They advise government and represent it in court, but must defend the rule of law even when it is inconvenient for the government.
examples: A President pressures the Attorney-General to give a legal opinion that an unconstitutional policy is lawful. The Attorney-General's constitutional duty is to uphold the rule of law and public interest — they must give honest legal advice, even when it is not what the President wants to hear.

The government is sued by a public interest organisation over a policy decision. The Attorney-General represents the national government in that court case — defending the government's legal position before the court.

title: Director of Public Prosecutions
original_text: There is established the office of Director of Public Prosecutions. The DPP has power to institute and undertake criminal proceedings against any person and can direct the Inspector-General of Police to investigate any matter. The DPP is not under the direction or control of any person or authority. The DPP serves a single non-renewable term of 8 years.
what_this_means: The DPP decides who gets prosecuted for crimes in Kenya — independently and without political interference. No President, minister, or court can tell the DPP who to prosecute or not. And to protect that independence, the DPP serves only one 8-year term and cannot be reappointed.
examples: A minister calls the DPP and instructs them to drop criminal charges against a political ally. The DPP is constitutionally independent — they are not subject to direction or control by any person, and must make prosecution decisions based on the law and public interest alone.

The DPP directs the Inspector-General of Police to investigate a senior government official for financial crimes. The police must comply — the DPP has constitutional authority to direct investigations, and police compliance is not optional.

title: Removal and resignation of Director of Public Prosecutions
original_text: The DPP may be removed only on grounds of inability to perform functions, non-compliance with Chapter 6, bankruptcy, incompetence, or gross misconduct. Removal requires a petition to the Public Service Commission, review by the President, and investigation by a tribunal appointed by the President.
what_this_means: The DPP is protected from politically motivated removal — they can only be removed through a formal process involving specific grounds, a petition, and an independent tribunal. A government unhappy with prosecutorial decisions cannot simply fire the DPP.
examples: The government tries to remove the DPP after he charges several Cabinet Secretaries with corruption. Removal requires a formal petition on specific constitutional grounds, a Public Service Commission review, and a presidential tribunal — a politically motivated removal attempt would fail at multiple steps.

The DPP is found to have issued false academic credentials during their appointment process. Non-compliance with Chapter 6 integrity requirements is a valid ground for removal — a petition can be legitimately filed and the process followed.

title: Judicial authority
original_text: Judicial authority is derived from the people and vests in the courts and tribunals established by the Constitution. In exercising judicial authority, courts shall be guided by: justice for all irrespective of status, no delay in justice, promotion of alternative dispute resolution, no undue procedural technicalities, and protection of the Constitution's principles.
what_this_means: Courts exist to deliver justice to everyone — rich or poor, powerful or ordinary. Justice must be quick, accessible, and not strangled by unnecessary procedural rules. Courts must also consider alternative ways of resolving disputes like mediation before insisting on a full trial.
examples: A court dismisses a valid legal case because the claimant used the wrong form when filing their papers, even though the substance of the case is clear. The Constitution requires courts not to be unreasonably restricted by procedural technicalities — a technical error alone should not defeat a legitimate claim.

Two neighbours have a land boundary dispute. Before taking it to court, they try community mediation. The Constitution encourages this — alternative dispute resolution is explicitly promoted as a legitimate way to resolve conflicts.

title: Independence of the Judiciary
original_text: The Judiciary shall be subject only to the Constitution and the law, and shall not be subject to the control or direction of any person or authority. The office of a judge shall not be abolished while there is a substantive holder. Judges' remuneration shall not be varied to their disadvantage.
what_this_means: No President, politician, or government can tell a judge how to decide a case. The judiciary answers only to the law and the Constitution. Judges' positions are also protected — you cannot fire a judge or cut their pay to punish them for inconvenient rulings.
examples: A powerful Cabinet Secretary calls a judge and suggests how a case involving his ministry should be decided. This is unconstitutional interference with judicial independence — the judge must report the call and decide the case purely on its legal merits.

Parliament passes a law cutting judges' salaries by 50% after judges issued several rulings against the government. This is unconstitutional — remuneration and benefits payable to judges cannot be varied to their disadvantage.

title: Judicial offices and officers
original_text: The Judiciary consists of judges of the superior courts, magistrates, other judicial officers and staff. The Judiciary has a Chief Justice (Head of the Judiciary), Deputy Chief Justice, and Chief Registrar. The Judicial Service Commission may establish other registrar offices.
what_this_means: Kenya's judicial system is structured with the Chief Justice at the top, followed by the Deputy CJ, then judges of various courts, magistrates, and judicial staff. The Chief Registrar manages the administrative running of the judiciary.
examples: A petitioner wants to understand who runs Kenya's court system administratively. The Chief Registrar is the chief administrator of the judiciary — handling administrative and financial matters so judges can focus on deciding cases.

A new court is being established and needs registrars to manage its administrative functions. The Judicial Service Commission has the authority to create registrar offices as needed — it does not need parliamentary approval for administrative judicial offices.

title: System of courts
original_text: The superior courts are the Supreme Court, the Court of Appeal, the High Court, and courts with High Court status for employment/labour and environment/land disputes. The subordinate courts are those established under Article 169 or by Parliament.
what_this_means: Kenya has a clear court hierarchy. At the top is the Supreme Court, then the Court of Appeal, then the High Court. For specialised matters, there are the Employment and Labour Relations Court and the Environment and Land Court, both with High Court status.
examples: A worker is dismissed unfairly and wants to sue her employer. She should go to the Employment and Labour Relations Court — a specialised court with the same status as the High Court, specifically set up for employment disputes.

A community has a major dispute over land title and compensation. The Environment and Land Court is the specialised court for land disputes — filing in the regular High Court on a land matter may result in the case being transferred.

title: Supreme Court
original_text: There is established the Supreme Court, consisting of the Chief Justice (president of the court), the Deputy Chief Justice (vice-president), and five other judges. The Supreme Court has exclusive original jurisdiction over presidential election disputes and appellate jurisdiction from the Court of Appeal and other courts prescribed by law. All courts are bound by Supreme Court decisions.
what_this_means: The Supreme Court is Kenya's highest court. It is the only court that can hear disputes about presidential elections, and its decisions bind every other court in the country. You can appeal to the Supreme Court from the Court of Appeal, especially on constitutional matters.
examples: A presidential election petition is filed in the High Court by mistake. The Constitution gives the Supreme Court exclusive jurisdiction over presidential election disputes — the petition must be in the Supreme Court, not any other court.

A Court of Appeal ruling on a constitutional matter creates confusion across other courts. A Supreme Court ruling on the same constitutional question settles it definitively — all courts, including the Court of Appeal, must follow Supreme Court decisions.

title: Court of Appeal
original_text: There is established the Court of Appeal, consisting of at least 12 judges. The Court of Appeal has jurisdiction to hear appeals from the High Court and other courts or tribunals as prescribed by an Act of Parliament.
what_this_means: The Court of Appeal is the second highest court in Kenya. If you lose a case in the High Court and believe the judge made a legal error, you can appeal to the Court of Appeal to have the decision reviewed.
examples: A person loses their property rights case in the High Court and believes the judge misapplied the law. They can appeal to the Court of Appeal, which will review whether the High Court's legal reasoning was correct — not just whether you liked the outcome.

A criminal convicted by the High Court believes the sentence was excessive or the conviction was wrong. They have the right to appeal to the Court of Appeal — this is a fundamental part of the right to a fair trial protected in the Bill of Rights.

title: High Court
original_text: The High Court has unlimited original jurisdiction in criminal and civil matters, jurisdiction to determine Bill of Rights violations, to hear appeals from lower courts and tribunals, and to interpret the Constitution. The High Court has supervisory jurisdiction over subordinate courts.
what_this_means: The High Court is the most powerful trial court in Kenya. It can hear any case — criminal or civil — without limits on the value or nature of the claim. It is also the primary court for enforcing your constitutional rights and for interpreting the Constitution when there is a dispute.
examples: A person believes their right to privacy has been violated by a government agency. They go directly to the High Court — it has jurisdiction to determine whether a Bill of Rights right has been violated and can order remedies.

A magistrate court delivers what appears to be a very unfair ruling in a case. The High Court has supervisory jurisdiction over subordinate courts — it can review the magistrate's decision to ensure it was legally sound.

title: Appointment of Chief Justice, Deputy Chief Justice and other judges
original_text: The President appoints the Chief Justice and Deputy Chief Justice following Judicial Service Commission recommendation and National Assembly approval. All other judges are appointed on JSC recommendation alone. Judges must have at minimum 10-15 years of relevant legal experience and high moral character.
what_this_means: Judges do not get their positions through political patronage — they go through a merit-based process managed by the Judicial Service Commission. The most senior judges (Chief Justice and Deputy CJ) also require Parliamentary approval, giving citizens a further check on who leads the judiciary.
examples: The President wants to appoint a personal friend as Chief Justice without going through the Judicial Service Commission process. This is unconstitutional — the Chief Justice appointment must follow the JSC's recommendation and receive National Assembly approval.

A person with only 5 years of legal practice applies to be appointed as a High Court judge. The Constitution requires at least 10 years of relevant experience — the candidate is not qualified regardless of other factors.

title: Tenure of office of the Chief Justice and other judges
original_text: A judge shall retire at age 70 but may choose to retire from age 65. The Chief Justice holds office for a maximum of 10 years or until the retirement age of 70, whichever comes first. Judges may resign by written notice to the President.
what_this_means: Judges have guaranteed tenure until age 70, protecting them from political pressure. The Chief Justice has an additional limit of 10 years in the top role. A judge cannot be forced out just because the government dislikes their rulings.
examples: The government tries to pass a law forcing all judges over 65 to retire. The Constitution sets retirement at 70 — judges can choose to retire at 65 but cannot be compelled to until 70. The law would be unconstitutional.

A Chief Justice is appointed at age 58. Even if they would not reach 70 for 12 more years, their maximum term as Chief Justice is 10 years — they must step down from the CJ role after 10 years, though they can continue as a regular Supreme Court judge.

title: Removal from office
original_text: A judge may be removed only on grounds of incapacity, breach of judicial code of conduct, bankruptcy, incompetence, or gross misconduct. Removal is initiated by the Judicial Service Commission or a public petition to the JSC. The President then appoints a tribunal to investigate, and acts on the tribunal's binding recommendations.
what_this_means: Judges can only be removed through a rigorous independent process — not because a politician dislikes a ruling. A tribunal investigates, makes binding recommendations, and the judge can appeal to the Supreme Court. This multi-step process protects judicial independence while still holding judges accountable.
examples: A High Court judge is caught accepting a bribe to alter a court ruling. This is gross misconduct — a petition can be filed with the Judicial Service Commission, which initiates the removal process. The tribunal will investigate and make binding recommendations.

The government tries to fast-track the removal of a judge who ruled against it in a high-profile case, alleging incompetence. The constitutional process requires a JSC investigation before a petition reaches the President — a politically motivated process will be exposed and challenged.

title: Subordinate courts
original_text: The subordinate courts are the Magistrates' courts, the Kadhis' courts, the Courts Martial, and any other court or local tribunal established by Parliament.
what_this_means: Below the High Court is the magistrate court — the most accessible court for most Kenyans, handling everyday criminal and civil cases. There are also specialised courts for Muslim family law (Kadhis' courts) and military discipline (Courts Martial).
examples: A resident of a rural town is accused of theft and the case is prosecuted by police. The case will be heard in the local magistrate's court — subordinate courts handle the vast majority of Kenya's criminal and civil matters at the grassroots level.

A Muslim couple in Mombasa disputes how their marriage property should be divided after divorce, both agreeing to resolve the matter under Islamic law. The Kadhis' court has jurisdiction to hear Muslim personal status matters where all parties are Muslim and consent to the court's jurisdiction.

title: Kadhis' courts
original_text: There shall be a Chief Kadhi and at least three other Kadhis. A Kadhi must profess the Muslim religion and have knowledge of Muslim law applicable to Muslim sects. Kadhis' courts jurisdiction is limited to questions of Muslim law relating to personal status, marriage, divorce or inheritance, where all parties profess the Muslim religion and submit to the court's jurisdiction.
what_this_means: Kadhis' courts handle Muslim family law matters — marriage, divorce, and inheritance — but only when all the people involved are Muslim and agree to use the court. A Muslim cannot be forced to use a Kadhi's court, and non-Muslims cannot be brought before one.
examples: A Muslim man wants his divorce processed by a Kadhi's court, but his wife prefers to use the civil courts. Because she does not consent to the Kadhi's court jurisdiction, the matter must be handled by the regular civil courts — the Kadhi's court requires all parties to consent.

A non-Muslim woman is in a dispute with a Muslim business partner about inheritance. This is a commercial matter, not a personal status issue, and it involves a non-Muslim — the Kadhi's court has no jurisdiction. The case goes to the regular civil courts.

title: Establishment of the Judicial Service Commission
original_text: There is established the Judicial Service Commission consisting of: the Chief Justice (chairperson), one Supreme Court judge, one Court of Appeal judge, one High Court judge and one magistrate, the Attorney-General, two advocates with 15+ years experience, one Public Service Commission nominee, and one man and one woman representing the public.
what_this_means: The Judicial Service Commission manages Kenya's judiciary — appointing judges, handling complaints against them, and disciplining or removing them. Its composition brings together judicial officers, advocates, and members of the public to ensure it is not controlled by any single group.
examples: A member of the public believes a magistrate in their town is consistently biased and takes bribes. They can file a complaint with the Judicial Service Commission, which has the authority to investigate judicial officers and take disciplinary action.

A new batch of High Court judges needs to be appointed. The JSC manages the entire competitive process — advertising the positions, interviewing candidates, evaluating their qualifications and character, and making recommendations to the President.

title: Functions of the Judicial Service Commission
original_text: The Judicial Service Commission shall promote and facilitate the independence and accountability of the judiciary. Its functions include recommending judicial appointments, reviewing conditions of service, appointing and disciplining magistrates and judicial officers, implementing judicial training programmes, and advising government on improving justice administration.
what_this_means: The JSC is both the guardian of judicial independence and the body responsible for judicial accountability. It balances ensuring judges are free from political interference while also ensuring they perform their duties properly and are held accountable when they fail.
examples: The Chief Justice notices that several magistrate courts have huge backlogs of cases because magistrates are not being productive. The JSC can investigate, provide training, and take disciplinary action against magistrates who are not performing their duties.

The government proposes a policy that would place the judiciary under executive oversight for budgeting purposes. The JSC can advise government that this would undermine judicial independence — one of its core constitutional functions.

title: Judiciary Fund
original_text: There is established a Judiciary Fund administered by the Chief Registrar. The Fund is used for administrative expenses of the Judiciary. Annual estimates of expenditure are prepared and submitted to the National Assembly for approval. Upon approval, judiciary expenditure is charged on the Consolidated Fund.
what_this_means: The judiciary has its own separate fund — it does not depend on ministry allocation for its budget. The Chief Registrar manages the money, and Parliament approves the annual budget. This financial independence supports judicial independence.
examples: The executive government threatens to withhold judiciary funding unless certain cases are decided in their favour. The Judiciary Fund is constitutionally established and its funding flows directly from the Consolidated Fund upon parliamentary approval — the executive cannot unilaterally withhold it.

The Chief Registrar prepares the judiciary's budget for the next financial year and submits it to the National Assembly. Parliament must consider and approve it — but cannot reduce it to a level that prevents the judiciary from functioning effectively.

title: Objects of devolution
original_text: The objects of devolution include: promoting democratic and accountable exercise of power; fostering national unity by recognising diversity; giving powers of self-governance to communities; recognising the right of communities to manage their affairs; protecting minority interests; promoting social and economic development; ensuring equitable sharing of resources; and enhancing checks and balances.
what_this_means: Devolution is not just about having 47 counties — it is about bringing power closer to you. The Constitution says decisions that affect your community should be made by your community, not by distant politicians in Nairobi. Devolution also ensures no single area monopolises national resources.
examples: Residents of a county argue that all major infrastructure decisions for their area should be made in Nairobi. Devolution was designed precisely to give communities the power to make decisions affecting them locally — county governments have constitutional authority over many such decisions.

A wealthy county with a large tax base gets the same equitable share allocation as a poor county, despite contributing more. The objects of devolution include equitable sharing of national and local resources throughout Kenya — poorer counties must receive fair support to develop.

title: Principles of devolved government
original_text: County governments shall be based on democratic principles and the separation of powers; shall have reliable sources of revenue to enable them to govern and deliver services effectively; and not more than two-thirds of the members of representative bodies in each county shall be of the same gender.
what_this_means: County governments must operate democratically with checks between the executive and assembly. Counties must have their own reliable money — not be financially starved by the national government. And the two-thirds gender rule applies at county level too.
examples: A county governor repeatedly ignores the county assembly and implements programmes without assembly approval. This violates the principle of separation of powers that must apply in counties — the assembly has legislative oversight authority that the governor cannot bypass.
A county assembly is elected with 70% male members. This exceeds the two-thirds gender limit — the same constitutional rule that applies in national Parliament applies in county assemblies.

title: County governments
original_text: There shall be a county government for each county, consisting of a county assembly and a county executive. Every county government shall decentralise its functions and the provision of its services to the extent that it is efficient and practicable to do so.
what_this_means: Each of Kenya's 47 counties has its own elected government — an assembly (like a mini-Parliament) and an executive headed by the governor. County governments must also bring services closer to people — they cannot centralise everything at the county headquarters.
examples: A county government runs all healthcare services from the county headquarters only, making it impossible for people in remote sub-counties to access services. The Constitution requires counties to decentralise service delivery to the extent practicable — this centralised approach can be challenged.

Residents in a ward of a large county have no access to any county government services. The county government's constitutional duty includes decentralising services — having sub-county and ward-level offices and service delivery points is part of fulfilling this duty.

title: Membership of county assembly
original_text: A county assembly consists of: members elected from single-member wards; special seat members to ensure no more than two-thirds are of the same gender; members representing marginalised groups including persons with disabilities and youth prescribed by Parliament; and the Speaker as an ex officio member.
what_this_means: Your county assembly has three layers of membership — your directly elected ward representative, additional special seat members to balance the gender ratio, and members representing persons with disabilities and youth. Every ward in Kenya has a directly elected MCA.
examples: Voters in Nyali Ward elect their Member of the County Assembly directly. This ward MCA is their primary representative at county level — bringing local issues to the county assembly and voting on county legislation and the county budget.

After ward elections in a county, the assembly is 72% male. Additional female special seat members must be nominated by political parties proportionally to bring the gender ratio within the two-thirds rule — this is constitutionally mandatory, not optional.

title: Speaker of a county assembly
original_text: Each county assembly shall have a speaker elected by the county assembly from among persons who are not members of the assembly. A sitting of the county assembly shall be presided over by the speaker or, in the speaker's absence, another member elected by the assembly.
what_this_means: County assembly speakers are elected by the assembly from people outside the assembly — just like Parliament's Speaker. This ensures the presiding officer is neutral and not simultaneously voting on legislation.
examples: A sitting Member of a County Assembly wants to be elected as Speaker of that same assembly without first resigning their ward seat. The Constitution requires the Speaker to be someone who is not a member of the assembly — the MCA must resign their ward seat before being eligible.

The Speaker of a county assembly is absent when a vote is called. Another member of the assembly is elected to preside for that session — county assembly proceedings can continue even when the Speaker is not present.

title: County executive committees
original_text: The executive authority of the county vests in and is exercised by a county executive committee, consisting of the county governor, deputy county governor, and members appointed by the governor with assembly approval. The governor and deputy are the chief executive and deputy chief executive of the county respectively.
what_this_means: The county governor is not a one-person ruler — they lead an executive committee that includes a deputy and other appointed members. Just like at national level, major executive decisions are collective, and the assembly must approve key appointments.
examples: A county governor makes all executive decisions personally without convening or consulting the county executive committee. The Constitution vests executive authority in the committee, not the governor alone — bypassing the committee for major decisions is unconstitutional.

The governor appoints a county executive committee member without seeking county assembly approval. The Constitution requires assembly approval for committee appointments — the appointment is not valid until approved.

title: Election of county governor and deputy county governor
original_text: The county governor is directly elected by voters registered in the county. Each gubernatorial candidate must nominate a deputy governor running mate. The IEBC declares the running mate of the elected governor as the elected deputy governor. No person may hold office as governor or deputy governor for more than two terms.
what_this_means: Governors are elected directly by county voters — you vote for your governor personally, not through representatives. The governor and deputy run together as a pair. And just like the President, governors are limited to two terms.
examples: A governor who has served two full terms wants to run for governor again in a different county. The Constitution's two-term limit on county governors is not county-specific — a person cannot hold office as governor for more than two terms total, regardless of which county.

A county governor wins a second term. Their running mate is automatically declared elected deputy governor without a separate election — voters chose the governor, and the deputy comes as part of the package, just as in a presidential election.

title: Removal of a county governor
original_text: A county governor may be removed on grounds of gross violation of the Constitution or other law, serious reasons for believing the governor has committed a crime under national or international law, abuse of office or gross misconduct, or physical or mental incapacity. Parliament shall enact legislation providing for the procedure.
what_this_means: A county governor can be removed — but only through a formal legal process based on specific serious grounds. Removing a governor for personal or political reasons without following the proper process is unconstitutional.
examples: A governor is caught misappropriating county funds for personal use. This constitutes abuse of office — the county assembly can initiate removal proceedings under the legislated procedure, leading to the governor's removal if the process is successfully completed.

County assembly members try to remove a governor because they dislike his development priorities. Disagreement over policy is not a constitutional ground for removal — only the specific grounds listed in this article can support a removal process.

title: Vacancy in the office of county governor
original_text: The office of county governor becomes vacant on death, resignation, loss of eligibility, criminal conviction with 12+ months imprisonment, or removal. On vacancy, the deputy county governor assumes office as county governor for the remainder of the term. If both positions are vacant, the county assembly speaker acts as governor and a fresh election must be held within 60 days.
what_this_means: If a governor's seat falls vacant, the deputy immediately steps up — just like national government succession. If both governor and deputy are gone simultaneously, the county assembly speaker temporarily takes over while a new election is organised.
examples: A county governor is convicted of fraud and sentenced to 18 months in prison. Because the conviction carries imprisonment of more than 12 months, the governor's office becomes vacant — the deputy automatically becomes governor.

Both the governor and deputy governor of a county are killed in an accident. The speaker of the county assembly assumes the governorship temporarily, and a fresh county gubernatorial election must be held within 60 days.

title: Functions of county executive committees
original_text: A county executive committee shall implement county legislation, implement national legislation within the county as required, manage and coordinate county administration and departments, and perform any other functions conferred by the Constitution or national legislation. The committee may prepare proposed legislation for the county assembly and shall provide the assembly with full and regular reports.
what_this_means: The county executive committee implements laws — both county-made laws and national laws that require local implementation. They also manage county administration and must regularly report to the county assembly on what they are doing with public resources.
examples: A county executive committee refuses to present its quarterly report to the county assembly, claiming the assembly has no right to that information. The Constitution explicitly requires the executive committee to provide the assembly with full and regular reports — this refusal is unconstitutional.

The national government passes a law requiring counties to implement a new maternal health programme. The county executive committee must implement this national legislation within its county — they cannot simply ignore national laws because they prefer their own programmes.

title: Urban areas and cities
original_text: National legislation shall provide for the governance and management of urban areas and cities and shall establish criteria for classifying areas as urban, principles of governance, and provide for participation by residents in governance of urban areas.
what_this_means: Cities and towns need special governance structures to deal with their unique challenges — Parliament must pass laws defining what qualifies as a city or town and how residents can participate in governing their urban area.
examples: Residents of a rapidly growing town want to know who is responsible for their urban planning and waste management. The Constitution requires national legislation to establish the governance structure for urban areas — this clarity helps residents know who to hold accountable.

A large urban area gets classified as a city under national legislation. The residents of that city must be given meaningful ways to participate in decisions affecting their city — including urban planning, public transport, and local services.

title: Legislative authority of county assemblies
original_text: The legislative authority of a county vests in and is exercised by its county assembly. A county assembly may make any laws necessary for the effective performance of county functions. A county assembly may exercise oversight over the county executive committee.
what_this_means: County assemblies make laws — real laws, with legal force in the county. They also oversee the governor's executive committee, just as Parliament oversees the national executive. MCAs are not just rubber stamps; they have genuine legislative and oversight power.
examples: A county assembly passes a by-law requiring all businesses in the county to register locally and pay a county business levy. This is valid county legislation — the assembly has authority to make laws necessary for county government functions.

The county assembly summons the governor's economic advisor to explain a controversial contract awarded to a foreign company. This is the assembly exercising its constitutional oversight function — county executives must account to the assembly for their decisions.

title: Respective functions and powers of national and county governments
original_text: The functions and powers of the national government and county governments are as set out in the Fourth Schedule. A function assigned to more than one level of government falls within concurrent jurisdiction of those levels. A function not assigned to a county is a function of the national government.
what_this_means: The Constitution clearly divides responsibilities between the national government and counties. Some functions are purely national (like defence), some are purely county (like local markets), and some are shared. When it is not clear who is responsible, the national government has the residual power.
examples: A county government decides to establish its own foreign policy and begin negotiating trade deals with foreign countries. Foreign affairs is a national government function — counties have no constitutional authority in that area.

A dispute arises over who is responsible for a public hospital — the national or county government. County health services (county hospitals and clinics) are a county function, but national referral hospitals remain a national function. The Fourth Schedule resolves such disputes.

title: Transfer of functions and powers between levels of government
original_text: A function or power of government at one level may be transferred to the other level by agreement between the governments if the function would be more effectively performed by the receiving government and the transfer is not prohibited by legislation. Resources necessary to perform the transferred function must also be transferred.
what_this_means: National and county governments can agree to transfer functions between themselves — but the money to perform those functions must move with them. A county cannot be given more responsibilities without also receiving the resources needed to carry them out.
examples: The national government agrees to transfer road maintenance responsibilities for certain county roads to a county government. The county must also receive the allocated budget for that road maintenance — the Constitution requires resources to follow functions.

A county asks to take over a function currently managed by the national government. The transfer is possible through a formal agreement — but if the law governing that function prohibits transfer, it cannot happen regardless of what both governments agree.

title: Boundaries of counties
original_text: The boundaries of a county may be altered only by a resolution recommended by an independent commission set up by Parliament and passed by two-thirds of the National Assembly and two-thirds of the county delegations in the Senate.
what_this_means: County boundaries cannot be changed by politicians on a whim. Any change requires an independent commission's recommendation plus a super-majority in both Houses of Parliament. This protects communities from having their county territories arbitrarily redrawn.
examples: The national government announces a plan to redraw boundaries between two counties by presidential decree. This is unconstitutional — county boundary changes require an independent commission recommendation and a super-majority vote in both Houses of Parliament.

Residents of a border area between two counties petition for their area to be moved from one county to another. The process requires Parliament to set up an independent commission, and both Houses must approve the change by two-thirds majorities.

title: Cooperation between national and county governments
original_text: Government at either level shall respect the functional and institutional integrity of the other level, assist, support and consult each other, and liaise for information exchange and policy coordination. Governments shall cooperate in performing shared functions and may set up joint committees.
what_this_means: The national government and county governments must work as partners — not as a boss and subordinate. The national government cannot ignore or undermine county governments, and counties must equally work within the national framework. Disputes must be resolved through negotiation, not confrontation.
examples: The national government begins implementing a major water project in a county without informing or consulting the county government. The Constitution requires governments to consult each other — implementing major projects in a county without county government involvement is unconstitutional.

A county government and the national government set up a joint committee to coordinate the rollout of a universal health coverage programme in the county. This is exactly the type of cooperative structure the Constitution envisions for shared functions.

title: Support for county governments
original_text: Parliament shall ensure county governments have adequate support to perform their functions. County governments shall operate compliant financial management systems. Parliament may authorise national government intervention if a county government is unable to perform its functions or fails to meet financial management requirements.
what_this_means: The national government must support counties so they can do their jobs properly. But if a county completely fails to function or manage money responsibly, the national government can step in — though this must follow a specific process with Senate oversight.
examples: A county government repeatedly fails to pay health workers' salaries and medical facilities run out of supplies. The national government can intervene to ensure services continue — but the Senate must be involved and the intervention must be proportionate and aimed at helping the county recover.

A county requests technical support from the national government for implementing a complex infrastructure project. The Constitution requires national government to support county governments — providing technical assistance is a legitimate and expected form of support.

title: Conflict of laws
original_text: National legislation prevails over county legislation if the national legislation applies uniformly throughout Kenya and: the matter cannot be regulated effectively by individual counties; uniformity across the nation is required; or it is necessary for national security, economic unity, or protection of the environment. Where neither condition applies, county legislation prevails.
what_this_means: When national and county laws conflict, there are clear rules for who wins. National law wins for big-picture national matters. County law wins for local matters where national uniformity is not needed. Courts must always try to find an interpretation that avoids a conflict.
examples: A county passes a by-law setting its own standards for food imports that differ from national standards. Because consumer protection and food standards require national uniformity to be effective, the national law prevails over the county by-law.

A county passes a law about management of local markets that differs slightly from national market management guidelines. Because this is a local matter where national uniformity is not required, the county law may prevail over the national guidelines.

title: Suspension of a county government
original_text: The President may suspend a county government in an emergency arising from internal conflict or war, or in other exceptional circumstances — but only if an independent commission has investigated allegations, the President is satisfied they are justified, and the Senate has authorised the suspension. Suspension cannot exceed 90 days and the Senate may terminate it at any time.
what_this_means: The national government cannot simply dissolve a county government it dislikes. Suspension requires an independent investigation, presidential satisfaction that the allegations are justified, and Senate approval. Even then, it lasts no more than 90 days.
examples: The President suspends a county government that has been vocal in opposing national policies, citing 'exceptional circumstances.' Without an independent commission's investigation and Senate approval, the suspension is unconstitutional — political opposition is not a ground for suspension.

A county government completely collapses due to internal factional fighting that disrupts service delivery. The national government, after an independent commission investigates and the Senate approves, can suspend the county for up to 90 days while stabilising the situation.

title: Qualifications for election as member of county assembly
original_text: A person is eligible for election to a county assembly if registered as a voter, meets educational and ethical requirements, and is nominated by a party or is an independent candidate supported by at least 500 registered voters in the ward. Disqualifications mirror those for parliamentary elections including being a public officer, unsound mind, bankrupt, or serving imprisonment of 6+ months.
what_this_means: To be a Member of the County Assembly, you need 500 ward voter signatures if running as an independent — much lower than Parliament — reflecting that county assembly members represent smaller, local communities.
examples: A young community leader in a ward wants to run as an MCA. If running as an independent, she needs signatures from at least 500 registered voters in her ward. This is a much more achievable threshold than national Parliament — it is designed to keep county assembly accessible to community-level candidates.

A ward officer working in the county government wants to contest the county assembly seat for his ward. He must resign from the county government position first — public officers are disqualified from standing for election while in office.

title: Vacation of office of member of county assembly
original_text: The office of an MCA becomes vacant on death, 8 unexcused absences, removal under the Constitution, resignation, party defection (or joining a party if elected as independent), end of assembly term, or becoming disqualified.
what_this_means: MCAs can lose their seats for the same reasons as MPs — including abandoning their party if they were elected on that party's ticket. If you were elected as an independent MCA and then join a political party, you immediately lose your seat.
examples: An MCA elected as a party candidate publicly resigns from that party and declares support for a rival. Under the Constitution, leaving the party you were elected on causes you to lose your county assembly seat — the vacancy must be filled.

An MCA misses 10 consecutive sittings without seeking the Speaker's permission and provides no acceptable reason. The seat becomes vacant — county assembly members have a constitutional obligation to attend and participate in assembly business.

title: County assembly power to summon witnesses
original_text: A county assembly and its committees have power to summon any person to appear before it for evidence or information. For this purpose, the assembly has the same powers as the High Court to enforce attendance of witnesses, compel documents, and examine witnesses.
what_this_means: County assembly committees have the same power to summon witnesses as the High Court. A county official, contractor, or any person cannot simply refuse to appear before a county assembly committee that has summoned them.
examples: A county assembly committee investigating irregular tender awards summons a private contractor to explain how they got the contract. The contractor cannot simply ignore the summons — the county assembly has High Court-equivalent powers to compel attendance.

A county executive committee member refuses to produce financial records requested by the county assembly. The assembly can use its power to compel production of documents — just as a High Court can order document disclosure.

title: Public participation and county assembly powers, privileges and immunities
original_text: A county assembly shall conduct its business openly, with sittings and committee sittings in public, and shall facilitate public participation and involvement in its legislative and other business. The public may only be excluded in exceptional circumstances.
what_this_means: County assembly proceedings must be open to the public — MCAs cannot legislate in secret. Citizens have the constitutional right to participate in their county assembly's work, not just to watch from the gallery.
examples: A county assembly holds budget discussions in a private session without notifying or inviting the public. This is unconstitutional — county assembly business must be conducted openly and the public must be able to participate, especially on major matters like the budget.

Residents of a county want to submit their views on a proposed county education policy before the assembly passes it into law. They have a constitutional right to participate — the assembly must create mechanisms for public input into legislation.

title: County assembly gender balance and diversity
original_text: Not more than two-thirds of the members of any county assembly or county executive committee shall be of the same gender. Parliament shall enact legislation to ensure county community and cultural diversity is reflected in the assembly and to prescribe mechanisms to protect minorities within counties.
what_this_means: The two-thirds gender rule applies everywhere in county government — both in the assembly and in the executive committee. County governments must also reflect the ethnic and cultural diversity within their county, not be dominated by one community.
examples: A county governor appoints an executive committee in which 9 of 10 members are from the same ethnic community as the governor. The Constitution requires the committee to reflect the county's community and cultural diversity — this composition can be legally challenged.

After county assembly elections, the gender balance is corrected through special seat nominations to ensure not more than two-thirds of members are of the same gender. This is the constitutional mechanism — the two-thirds rule must be achieved, not just attempted.

title: County government during transition
original_text: While an election is being held to constitute a county assembly, the executive committee as last constituted remains competent to perform administrative functions until a new executive committee is constituted after the election.
what_this_means: When a county goes to election, the old county executive does not immediately vacate — they stay on in a caretaker capacity to keep the county functioning until a new government is formed after the election.
examples: On election day in a county, the governor and executive committee continue to perform their administrative functions even as voting happens. They are in caretaker mode — they can run day-to-day operations but cannot make major new decisions during this transitional period.

A county assembly is dissolved before a general election but essential county services like hospitals and schools must continue operating. The existing executive committee maintains administrative functions — essential services do not shut down during election transitions.

title: Publication of county legislation
original_text: County legislation does not take effect unless published in the Gazette. National and county legislation may prescribe additional requirements for publication of county legislation.
what_this_means: A county law only becomes effective and binding once it is published in the official Gazette. A county assembly can pass a law, but it has no force until gazetted — residents cannot be expected to comply with laws that are not publicly published.
examples: A county governor begins enforcing a new county by-law that the assembly passed but that has not yet been gazetted. Enforcement of an un-gazetted law is unlawful — the by-law has no legal effect until it is published in the Gazette.

A business owner is fined for violating a county regulation. She checks and finds the regulation has never been published in the Gazette. The fine is invalid — county legislation that has not been gazetted has no legal effect.

title: Legislation on Chapter
original_text: Parliament shall enact legislation providing for all matters necessary or convenient to give effect to this Chapter, including governance of the capital city, transfer of functions between levels of government, election and appointment of county officers, and suspension of assemblies and executive committees.
what_this_means: Parliament must pass detailed laws to make the devolution chapter fully operational. Without these enabling laws, many provisions of devolution remain aspirational — Parliament has a constitutional duty to make devolution real and workable.
examples: Disputes arise about how to govern Nairobi City given that it is both a county and the national capital. Parliament is constitutionally required to pass legislation specifically for the governance of the capital city — citizens can push for this legislation if Parliament delays.

A county government and national government disagree about which functions have been transferred to the county and what resources should follow. Parliament must enact clear legislation on the transfer of functions and accompanying resources — ambiguity on this point is a legislative failure.

title: Principles of public finance
original_text: All public finance shall be guided by: openness and accountability including public participation; promotion of an equitable society where the burden of taxation is shared fairly and revenue shared equitably between national and county governments; sharing burdens and benefits of resources equitably between generations; prudent use of public money; and clear fiscal reporting.
what_this_means: Kenya's public money must be managed openly, fairly, and responsibly. Tax burdens must be shared fairly — the rich should pay more than the poor. Revenue must be distributed equitably between national and county levels. And future generations should not be burdened by today's reckless borrowing.
examples: The national government borrows excessively to fund projects with no clear repayment plan, saddling future Kenyans with unmanageable debt. The Constitution requires that burdens of public borrowing be shared equitably between present and future generations — this reckless borrowing violates that principle.

A county government spends public money without publishing expenditure reports or allowing public scrutiny. The Constitution requires openness, accountability, and public participation in financial matters — closed financial management at county level is unconstitutional.

title: Equitable sharing of national revenue
original_text: Revenue raised nationally shall be shared equitably among the national and county governments. County governments may be given additional allocations from the national government's share, either conditionally or unconditionally.
what_this_means: The money Kenya collects in taxes and other revenue must be shared fairly between the national government and the 47 counties. Counties can also receive extra allocations — with or without conditions attached — beyond their basic share.
examples: The national government collects KES 2 trillion in revenue. A constitutionally defined equitable share — at least 15% — must be transferred to the county governments to fund their services. The national government cannot retain all of it.

A county that hosts a major national park is given an additional unconditional allocation to compensate communities for conservation restrictions on their land. This additional allocation on top of the equitable share is constitutionally permitted.

title: Equitable share and other financial laws
original_text: The equitable share allocation shall take into account: national interest, public debt, national government needs, county government capacity to perform functions, fiscal capacity of counties, developmental needs, economic disparities, affirmative action for disadvantaged areas, economic optimisation, stable and predictable allocations, and flexibility for emergencies. County governments shall receive at least 15% of nationally collected revenue.
what_this_means: At minimum, 15% of all nationally collected revenue must go to counties — this is a constitutional floor, not a ceiling. The actual allocation is decided based on multiple criteria including counties' needs, capacity, and development gaps.
examples: Parliament passes a Division of Revenue Bill allocating only 12% of national revenue to counties. This is unconstitutional — the Constitution guarantees counties at least 15%, and Parliament cannot pass a bill that violates this floor.

A sparsely populated arid county with few economic resources receives a larger share than a more developed county. The allocation criteria include economic disparities and developmental needs — poorer, less developed counties are entitled to larger allocations to help them catch up.

title: Equalisation Fund
original_text: There is established an Equalisation Fund into which 0.5% of national revenue is paid annually. The Fund is used to provide basic services — water, roads, health facilities, electricity — to marginalised areas, to bring them to the standard enjoyed by the rest of the nation. The Fund lapses 20 years after the Constitution's effective date unless Parliament extends it.
what_this_means: Half a percent of all Kenya's tax revenue is set aside specifically to bring basic services to marginalised areas — arid regions, remote communities, historically neglected parts of the country. This is targeted development funding, constitutionally guaranteed.
examples: A remote community in Mandera has no health facility and no clean water supply. The Equalisation Fund is constitutionally designed to fund exactly this — water and health facilities in marginalised areas to bring them to the level the rest of Kenya enjoys.

The government uses Equalisation Fund money to build a road in Nairobi rather than in marginalised areas. This is a constitutional violation — the Fund can only be used in marginalised areas and specifically for basic services like water, roads, health, and electricity.

title: Consultation on financial legislation affecting counties
original_text: When a Bill dealing with sharing of revenue or financial matters concerning county governments is published, the Commission on Revenue Allocation shall consider those provisions and may make recommendations to the National Assembly and the Senate. Each House shall consider the recommendations before voting on the Bill.
what_this_means: Before Parliament votes on any law about money and counties, the Commission on Revenue Allocation must be given the chance to review it and make recommendations. Parliament must then actually consider those recommendations before voting — they cannot be ignored.
examples: Parliament rushes through a Division of Revenue Bill without giving the Commission on Revenue Allocation time to review and comment on it. This is a procedural constitutional violation — the Commission must be consulted, and Parliament must consider their recommendations.

The Commission on Revenue Allocation reviews a proposed bill on county revenue and recommends changes to better protect small counties. Parliament must table and consider these recommendations before the vote — though Parliament can ultimately decide differently, it cannot pretend the recommendations do not exist.

title: Consolidated Fund and other public funds
original_text: There is established the Consolidated Fund into which all national government revenue is paid, except money excluded by law for specific purposes. Money may only be withdrawn from the Consolidated Fund with parliamentary appropriation or as charged by the Constitution. Withdrawal requires Controller of Budget approval.
what_this_means: All national government money goes into one central fund — the Consolidated Fund. Nothing can be taken from it without Parliament saying so through an appropriation, or without the Controller of Budget approving the withdrawal. This prevents secret or unauthorised spending.
examples: A ministry begins spending money on an unapproved project by drawing directly from government accounts without a parliamentary appropriation. This is unconstitutional — no money can be withdrawn from the Consolidated Fund without either parliamentary appropriation or a specific constitutional charge.

The Controller of Budget refuses to approve a withdrawal from the Consolidated Fund for a project that lacks proper documentation and authorisation. The Constitution empowers the Controller to do exactly this — financial discipline depends on this gatekeeping function.

title: Revenue Funds for county governments
original_text: There shall be established a Revenue Fund for each county government into which all county revenue is paid. Money may only be withdrawn from a county Revenue Fund as charged by law or as authorised by the county assembly. The Controller of Budget must approve all withdrawals.
what_this_means: Just as there is a Consolidated Fund for national government, each county has its own Revenue Fund. County money cannot be touched without the county assembly's authorisation and the Controller of Budget's approval.
examples: A county governor makes unilateral payments from the county revenue fund without county assembly approval. This is unconstitutional — withdrawals from the county Revenue Fund require either a legal charge or county assembly authorisation.

The Controller of Budget refuses to approve county fund withdrawals because the county has not submitted proper financial reports. The Controller's gatekeeping role applies at county level too — counties cannot bypass this constitutional requirement.

title: Contingencies Fund
original_text: There is established a Contingencies Fund. The Cabinet Secretary responsible for finance may authorise advances from the Fund if satisfied there is an urgent and unforeseen need for expenditure for which there is no other authority.
what_this_means: The Contingencies Fund is Kenya's emergency money — for urgent and genuinely unforeseen spending needs that Parliament could not have anticipated in the budget. It is not a slush fund for routine expenses that were simply not budgeted.
examples: A major flood disaster destroys infrastructure in multiple counties. The Cabinet Secretary for Finance can authorise an advance from the Contingencies Fund for emergency response — this is exactly the kind of urgent, unforeseen need it is designed for.

A ministry uses Contingencies Fund money to pay salaries that were simply not included in their budget, claiming it is 'urgent.' Routine expenditures that were foreseeable during budgeting do not qualify — the Fund is for genuinely unforeseen emergencies, not budget failures.

title: Power to impose taxes and charges
original_text: Only the national government may impose income tax, VAT, customs duties, and excise tax. Counties may impose property rates, entertainment taxes, and other taxes authorised by Parliament. Both levels may impose charges for services. County taxing powers shall not prejudice national economic policies.
what_this_means: Big national taxes — income tax, VAT, customs — are only for the national government. Counties can tax property and entertainment and charge for services, but they cannot create taxes that disrupt national economic policy or undermine businesses operating across county borders.
examples: A county governor announces that all goods entering the county from other counties will attract a 10% 'county import levy.' This is unconstitutional — counties cannot impose charges that disrupt national economic activities or restrict movement of goods across county boundaries.

A county government levies annual property rates on commercial buildings in the county. This is a constitutionally authorised county tax — property rates are explicitly listed as a county taxing power.

title: Imposition of tax
original_text: No tax or licensing fee may be imposed, waived or varied except as provided by legislation. If legislation permits waivers, a public record of each waiver and its reason shall be maintained and reported to the Auditor-General. No law may exempt a State officer from payment of tax by reason of their office.
what_this_means: Taxes can only be created, changed, or waived by law — no official can invent or remove a tax on their own. And State officers — politicians included — must pay taxes just like everyone else. No government job exempts you from taxation.
examples: A revenue officer waives taxes for a company on his personal decision, with no documentation and no legal authority to do so. This is unconstitutional — tax waivers must be authorised by legislation, properly recorded, and reported to the Auditor-General.

A senior government minister claims her office entitles her to exemption from income tax. No law can exempt State officers from tax based on their office — she must pay income tax just like every other Kenyan.

title: Borrowing by national government
original_text: Parliament may prescribe the terms on which the national government may borrow and impose reporting requirements. The Cabinet Secretary for Finance must present detailed information about any loan to the relevant Parliament committee within 7 days of a resolution requesting it.
what_this_means: The national government can borrow money — but Parliament sets the rules and must be informed about the details of any loan. Transparency about government borrowing is constitutionally required, not optional.
examples: Parliament passes a resolution requesting full details of a new government loan from China. The Cabinet Secretary for Finance must present all information — including repayment terms, interest rates, and what the money is for — within 7 days. She cannot delay or refuse.

The government borrows billions from an international institution under terms that Parliament has not approved or been informed of. This violates the constitutional requirement for parliamentary oversight of government borrowing.

title: Borrowing by counties
original_text: A county government may borrow only if the national government guarantees the loan and the county government's assembly approves the borrowing.
what_this_means: Counties cannot borrow money on their own initiative — national government must guarantee the loan and the county assembly must approve it. This prevents counties from accumulating unmanageable debts that they then cannot repay.
examples: A county governor takes out a commercial loan from a bank in the county government's name without county assembly approval or national government guarantee. This is unconstitutional — both conditions must be met before a county can legally borrow.
