A county wants to borrow to build a new hospital and goes through the proper process: gets national government agreement to guarantee the loan and tables the borrowing proposal in the county assembly for approval. This is the constitutionally correct procedure.

title: Loan guarantees by national government
original_text: An Act of Parliament shall prescribe terms and conditions for national government loan guarantees. Within two months after the end of each financial year, the national government shall publish a report on all guarantees given during that year.
what_this_means: When the national government guarantees a county or parastatal loan — meaning Kenya will repay it if the borrower defaults — Parliament must set the rules for when and how this happens. And an annual public report must disclose all guarantees given.
examples: The national government secretly guarantees a large loan for a parastatal without any parliamentary framework or disclosure. This is unconstitutional — Parliament must prescribe the terms for guarantees and the government must publish an annual report on all guarantees.

Citizens want to know which entities the government has guaranteed loans for and on what terms. The Constitution requires this to be published annually after the financial year — this information is public and citizens can demand access to it.

title: Public debt
original_text: The public debt is a charge on the Consolidated Fund. The public debt includes all financial obligations from loans raised or guaranteed and securities issued or guaranteed by the national government.
what_this_means: Kenya's national debt — everything borrowed or guaranteed by the national government — is automatically paid from the Consolidated Fund. This means debt repayment comes before other spending; it is a prior charge on national revenue.
examples: Kenya has a loan repayment due to an international bank. Even if Parliament has not passed an appropriation for it, the repayment must still happen — public debt is a charge on the Consolidated Fund that does not require a separate parliamentary vote each time.

The government takes on a new loan that raises total public debt significantly. Citizens and Parliament have the right to be informed — the public debt is a collective obligation of all Kenyans and transparency about its size and terms is constitutionally expected.

title: Commission on Revenue Allocation
original_text: There is established the Commission on Revenue Allocation consisting of a chairperson approved by the National Assembly, two persons nominated by National Assembly parties, five persons nominated by Senate parties, and the Principal Secretary for Finance.
what_this_means: The Commission on Revenue Allocation is the independent body that recommends how national revenue should be shared between the national government and the 47 counties. Its mixed composition — drawing from both houses and having technical expertise — ensures recommendations balance political and technical considerations.
examples: Counties feel the current revenue sharing formula disadvantages them. They can engage the Commission on Revenue Allocation — whose function is precisely to recommend fairer bases for equitable sharing — to review and recommend changes to the formula.

The government tries to change the revenue allocation formula without involving the CRA. This is unconstitutional — the Commission's recommendations must be part of any revenue allocation decision, and its independence is constitutionally protected.

title: Functions of the Commission on Revenue Allocation
original_text: The principal function of the Commission is to recommend the basis for equitable sharing of national revenue between national and county governments and among counties. It also advises on county government financing, defines marginalised areas, and submits recommendations to both Houses and to county governments.
what_this_means: The CRA's main job is to work out a fair formula for sharing money between national government and counties, and between different counties. It also identifies which areas count as marginalised for purposes of the Equalisation Fund.
examples: A county government wants to know why it received a smaller allocation than a neighbouring county. They can engage the CRA to understand the formula — the Commission publishes its criteria and recommendations openly for all county governments.

The CRA determines that a certain arid county qualifies as a 'marginalised area' for Equalisation Fund purposes based on its criteria. This determination enables that county's communities to access targeted additional funds for basic services.

title: Division of revenue
original_text: Once every five years, the Senate shall by resolution determine the basis for allocating among counties the share of national revenue annually allocated to county level. The Senate must consult county governors, the Finance Cabinet Secretary, and the public. The National Assembly may approve, amend (by two-thirds) or reject (by two-thirds) the resolution.
what_this_means: Every five years, the Senate decides the formula for dividing county revenue among the 47 counties. The National Assembly can review it but needs a two-thirds majority to change it or reject it — giving the Senate strong protection over county revenue allocation.
examples: The Senate is due to pass the five-year revenue allocation resolution. Before doing so, county governors, the public, and the Finance Cabinet Secretary must all be consulted — this is a constitutionally required process, not optional consultation.

The National Assembly wants to change the Senate's revenue allocation resolution by a simple majority vote of 51%. This is insufficient — changing the Senate's resolution requires at least two-thirds of all National Assembly members to vote for the amendment.

title: Annual Division of Revenue Bills
original_text: At least two months before the end of each financial year, the Cabinet Secretary responsible for finance shall introduce in the National Assembly a Division of Revenue Bill and County Allocation of Revenue Bill.
what_this_means: Every year, before the budget cycle ends, the Finance Cabinet Secretary must formally introduce two bills to Parliament — one dividing money between national and county governments, and one dividing the county share among all 47 counties. This is mandatory and must happen on time.
examples: It is March and the Finance CS has not yet introduced the Division of Revenue Bill to Parliament. This is a constitutional violation — the bill must be introduced at least two months before the end of the financial year in June.

A county governor complains that the County Allocation of Revenue Bill was tabled very late, giving counties insufficient time to plan their budgets. The Constitution requires both bills to be introduced with enough lead time — late tabling is unconstitutional.

title: Transfer of equitable share
original_text: A county's share of national revenue shall be transferred to the county without conditions, deductions or set-offs, except as provided for in the Constitution.
what_this_means: When national revenue is allocated to a county, the money must be transferred in full — the national government cannot hold it back, impose conditions on how it is spent, or deduct amounts without constitutional authority. County governments have a right to their full equitable share.
examples: The national government announces it will only release county funds if counties first submit detailed spending plans and get national approval. This is unconstitutional — county allocations must be transferred without conditions.

A county receives its equitable share transfer and notes that the national Treasury deducted a set-off for an old loan without any court order or constitutional provision allowing it. The county can legally challenge that deduction.

title: Budgets
original_text: The national government and county governments shall each prepare annual budgets. The national government budget must include national revenue and expenditure estimates, measures to ensure equitable sharing, and statements on actual and projected debt. County budgets must show county revenue and expenditure. Accounting standards shall be uniform.
what_this_means: Both national and county governments must produce proper annual budgets that are transparent and follow uniform accounting standards. Budgets must show real debt figures and how equity between regions is being maintained — not just optimistic estimates.
examples: A county government produces a budget that fails to show its actual debt obligations or how it plans to fund its programmes. The Constitution requires detailed and transparent budget documents — this county's budget does not meet constitutional standards.

The national budget is presented without any figures on actual or projected national debt. This violates the constitutional requirement that the national budget include a statement on debt — Parliament can demand a corrected budget document.

title: Budget estimates and parliamentary oversight
original_text: The Cabinet Secretary for Finance shall, at least two months before the end of each financial year, submit estimates of revenue and expenditure to the National Assembly. A committee shall consider the estimates and table recommendations. The National Assembly shall take into account the recommendations before debating and approving the budget.
what_this_means: The Finance Cabinet Secretary must give Parliament the budget estimates at least two months before the financial year ends. A parliamentary committee reviews these and makes recommendations, which Parliament must consider before voting on the budget. This ensures genuine scrutiny rather than rubber-stamping.
examples: The Finance CS submits budget estimates to Parliament only one month before the financial year ends. This violates the two-month minimum required by the Constitution — Parliament needs adequate time to review and make recommendations.

The National Assembly approves the budget without ever considering or debating the committee's recommendations. This is unconstitutional — Parliament must take the committee's findings into account before passing the budget.

title: Expenditure before the Appropriations Bill is passed
original_text: If the Appropriations Act for a financial year has not been passed by the beginning of that year, the Controller of Budget may authorise expenditure of up to half of the amounts in the previous year's Appropriations Act to enable the government to continue to function, until the new Act is passed.
what_this_means: If Parliament has not passed the budget before the new financial year starts, the government does not shut down. The Controller of Budget can authorise spending of up to 50% of last year's budget to keep essential services running — a constitutionally protected safety valve.
examples: Parliament is still debating the budget when July 1st arrives. The Controller of Budget can authorise government spending of up to half of the previous year's approved budget, so hospitals, schools, and civil servants continue to receive funds while Parliament concludes its debate.

A ministry wants to start a brand-new project not in the previous year's budget before the new Appropriations Act is passed. The Controller of Budget cannot authorise this — only continuation of existing spending up to 50% of last year's level is permitted.

title: Supplementary appropriation
original_text: A national government or county government may spend money that has not been appropriated only if the spending is authorised by the Cabinet Secretary for Finance (national) or county executive (county), is less than 10% of the total authorised for the relevant year, the relevant legislature ratifies it within two months, and total expenditure does not exceed the overall approved amount.
what_this_means: Governments can spend unbudgeted money in emergencies or for unforeseen needs — but only up to 10% of the total approved budget, and only if the legislature formally approves it within two months. This prevents unlimited emergency spending without accountability.
examples: After floods destroy bridges, a county government spends money from unappropriated funds to fund emergency repairs. This is lawful if the amount is within 10% of the approved budget and the county assembly ratifies the expenditure within two months.

A national ministry wants to spend money that exceeds 10% of its approved budget under a supplementary appropriation claim. This is unconstitutional — the 10% cap is a hard limit that cannot be exceeded without a new Appropriations Act.

title: County appropriation
original_text: Before a county government may spend money from its county revenue fund, the county assembly must approve an appropriation for that purpose.
what_this_means: County governments cannot spend any money from their county revenue fund without first getting county assembly approval. The elected representatives of the county — not the governor or executives alone — must authorise all spending.
examples: A county governor instructs the county treasury to pay a contractor from the county revenue fund before the county assembly has approved the expenditure. This is unconstitutional — no money may be spent without prior county assembly appropriation.

A county assembly passes its appropriation bill before the financial year begins, enabling the county government to legally start spending on approved programmes from the first day of the new year. This is the constitutionally correct process.

title: Financial legislation and interventions
original_text: Parliament shall enact legislation establishing financial controls, and providing for the financial management of public entities. The Cabinet Secretary for Finance may intervene in a county's finances if the county persistently and materially fails its financial management obligations.
what_this_means: Parliament must create laws governing how public money is managed. If a county government repeatedly and seriously mismanages its finances, the national government's Finance Cabinet Secretary has the power to step in — but only through a formal, legally prescribed process.
examples: A county government repeatedly fails to submit audited accounts, fails to pay civil servants, and accumulates unsustainable debt. After following the constitutionally prescribed process, the Finance Cabinet Secretary can intervene in the county's financial management.

The Finance Cabinet Secretary tries to take over a county's budget because she disagrees with the governor's spending priorities. This is not a valid basis for intervention — only persistent and material failure to meet financial management obligations justifies intervention.

title: Accounts and audit of public entities
original_text: Accounts of national, county and other public entities shall be audited by the Auditor-General. The person responsible for the accounts of a public entity is accountable to the relevant legislature for that entity's financial management.
what_this_means: Every public institution — national ministries, county governments, parastatals — must have their accounts audited by the independent Auditor-General. And the accounting officer — usually a Principal Secretary or equivalent — is personally accountable to Parliament or the county assembly for how money was managed.
examples: A county CEO refuses to allow the Auditor-General access to the county enterprise's financial records, claiming they are a private company. If public funds are involved, the Auditor-General has a constitutional right to audit those accounts.

The Auditor-General tables a report showing serious financial irregularities in a national ministry. The Principal Secretary of that ministry must appear before the relevant parliamentary committee and answer for those irregularities — they are personally accountable.

title: Procurement of public goods and services
original_text: When a State organ or other public entity contracts for goods or services, it shall do so in accordance with a system that is fair, equitable, transparent, competitive and cost-effective. An Act of Parliament shall prescribe a framework for procurement.
what_this_means: Government contracts must be awarded through a process that is fair, open, competitive, and gives value for taxpayers' money. No ministry or county can just pick any supplier it wants — procurement must follow rules that are designed to prevent corruption and favouritism.
examples: A county government awards a road construction contract to the governor's brother without any competitive tendering process. This violates the constitutional requirement for fair, transparent, and competitive procurement — it can be challenged in court.

A ministry invites bids for a project but sets qualification criteria so specific that only one company can qualify, effectively eliminating competition. This undermines the constitutional procurement principles of fairness and competitiveness.

title: Controller of Budget
original_text: There shall be a Controller of Budget appointed by the President with National Assembly approval. The Controller shall oversee the implementation of the budgets of national and county governments, and shall authorise withdrawals from public funds. The Controller reports to the National Assembly every four months.
what_this_means: The Controller of Budget is the independent official who ensures that money is only withdrawn from government accounts when it has been properly approved, and who tracks how both national and county budgets are actually being spent. Their quarterly reports give Parliament the power to catch problems early.
examples: The Controller of Budget's quarterly report reveals that a county government has spent 90% of its annual development budget in just the first four months of the year — mostly in suspicious payments. Parliament and the public can use this report to demand accountability.

A ministry tries to make a large withdrawal from the Consolidated Fund without proper appropriation documentation. The Controller of Budget must refuse to authorise the withdrawal — their oversight role is to prevent unauthorised withdrawals.

title: Auditor-General
original_text: There shall be an Auditor-General appointed by the President with National Assembly approval, for a single term of eight years. The Auditor-General shall audit and report on the accounts of national and county governments, courts, and all public entities, within six months of the end of each financial year.
what_this_means: The Auditor-General is Kenya's independent financial watchdog. They serve one non-renewable eight-year term — protecting them from political pressure — and must audit all public accounts and report to Parliament within six months of each financial year ending.
examples: The financial year ends in June and by October the Auditor-General has not yet tabled audit reports to Parliament. The Constitution requires the reports within six months — Parliament can formally demand them and the Auditor-General can be held accountable for the delay.

A new President tries to fire the Auditor-General for publishing a report exposing financial mismanagement in his administration. The Auditor-General's appointment process and single fixed term are designed precisely to prevent this kind of political interference.

title: Salaries and Remuneration Commission
original_text: There is established the Salaries and Remuneration Commission to set and regularly review the remuneration and benefits of all State officers, and advise the national and county governments on the remuneration of public officers.
what_this_means: The Salaries and Remuneration Commission independently sets and reviews the pay of all State officers — including the President, MPs, governors, and judges. No politician can set their own salary or allow Parliament to vote itself a pay rise — an independent commission does it.
examples: Members of Parliament push through a resolution doubling their own salaries. They cannot lawfully do this — the SRC has the exclusive constitutional mandate to set State officer remuneration. The resolution has no legal force.

A county governor complains that her salary has not kept up with inflation. She can formally petition the SRC to review county executive remuneration — this is the constitutionally correct process for adjusting State officer pay.

title: Central Bank of Kenya
original_text: There is established the Central Bank of Kenya. The Central Bank shall formulate monetary policy, promote price stability, issue currency, and perform other functions conferred by an Act of Parliament. The Central Bank is not subject to direction by any person or authority in the performance of its functions.
what_this_means: The Central Bank of Kenya is constitutionally independent. It controls monetary policy and currency — and no politician, President, or minister can direct it on how to exercise those functions. This independence protects Kenya's economy from political manipulation.
examples: The President's office issues a directive to the Central Bank to lower interest rates before an election to boost short-term economic activity. The Central Bank is constitutionally protected from such direction and can lawfully refuse to comply.

Parliament passes a law requiring the Central Bank to print money to fund government spending. The Central Bank's constitutional mandate of price stability and independence means it must resist directives that undermine monetary stability.

title: Values and principles of public service
original_text: The values and principles of public service include high standards of professional ethics; efficient, effective and economic use of resources; responsive, prompt and impartial delivery of services; representation of Kenya's diverse communities; affording citizens their rights and entitlements; provision of democratic, accountable service; and transparency and provision of timely, accurate information to citizens.
what_this_means: Public servants — from hospital nurses to immigration officers to county clerks — must serve all Kenyans with professionalism, speed, fairness, and accountability. Representing Kenya's diversity is not just a policy goal, it is a constitutional value binding on all public institutions.
examples: A government department consistently takes months to respond to citizen applications and when pressed, gives no explanation. This violates the constitutional public service values of responsiveness, promptness, and provision of accurate information to the public.

A national institution's senior staff are all drawn from one ethnic community. The Constitution requires that public service reflect Kenya's diverse communities — this lack of diversity is a constitutional concern that citizens and oversight bodies can raise.

title: Public Service Commission
original_text: There is a Public Service Commission consisting of a chairperson and twelve members appointed by the President with National Assembly approval. The Commission manages and oversees the national public service, and establishes and abolishes offices in the national government.
what_this_means: The PSC is the independent body responsible for hiring, managing, and disciplining civil servants in the national government. It creates and abolishes government positions — ensuring that public service appointments are based on merit rather than political patronage.
examples: A minister tries to directly hire a friend into a senior civil service role, bypassing the Public Service Commission. The PSC has constitutional authority over such appointments — the minister cannot make the appointment unilaterally.

The national government wants to create a new department to coordinate climate change programmes. The Public Service Commission must formally establish the office and define its structure — new government offices cannot just be created informally.

title: Functions and powers of the Public Service Commission
original_text: The functions of the PSC include: establishing and abolishing offices in the national public service; appointing persons to offices; handling promotions, transfers, dismissals and other employment matters; promoting the values in Article 232; and investigating violations of public service values.
what_this_means: The PSC does far more than just hire people — it also manages the entire lifecycle of civil service employment, investigates violations of public service standards, and actively promotes the constitutional values that all public servants must uphold.
examples: A citizen complains that a government official demanded a bribe before processing a service. The PSC has the power to investigate violations of public service values and can take disciplinary action against that officer.

A public servant is promoted over more qualified colleagues because of their ethnic background. The PSC is constitutionally mandated to handle promotions based on merit and to investigate breaches of the fairness and non-discrimination values.

title: County public service
original_text: A county government shall have a county public service and shall establish a county public service board. The county board shall manage county public service appointments, promotions and discipline, subject to constitutional values.
what_this_means: Each of the 47 counties has its own public service and its own public service board. County governments manage their own civil servants — hiring, promoting, and disciplining them — but must follow the same constitutional values of merit, fairness, and diversity that apply nationally.
examples: A county governor dismisses a county health officer who exposed procurement irregularities in his administration. The county public service board — not the governor — handles discipline, and such a dismissal would need to follow proper procedures and cannot be used to victimise whistleblowers.

A county public service board hires all new county officers from only one sub-county, ignoring candidates from other parts of the county. This violates the constitutional requirement that the public service reflect the diversity of the communities it serves.

title: Protection of public officers
original_text: A public officer shall not be dismissed, removed from office, demoted, victimised or disciplined by reason of their membership of a trade union or participation in legitimate union activities.
what_this_means: Public servants have a constitutional right to join trade unions and to participate in lawful union activities without fear of punishment. A government employer cannot fire, demote, or victimise a civil servant just because they are a union member or went on a lawful strike.
examples: A hospital matron is demoted after she helped organise health workers to formally petition management about poor working conditions through their union. This demotion is unconstitutional — participation in legitimate trade union activities is protected.

A government ministry threatens to dismiss any employee who attends a trade union meeting on their day off. The Constitution prohibits victimisation of public officers for union membership — this threat is unlawful.

title: National Cohesion and Integration
original_text: The national government shall promote national cohesion, integration and unity among Kenyans. The State shall not discriminate directly or indirectly against any person on ethnic or racial grounds in the conduct of public affairs.
what_this_means: The national government has a constitutional duty to actively bring Kenyans together across ethnic and racial lines. And in running public affairs, no person can be discriminated against because of their ethnicity or race — this applies to hiring, service delivery, and every aspect of how government operates.
examples: A government department systematically rejects job applicants from certain regions of Kenya, favouring candidates from the minister's home county. This is direct ethnic discrimination in public affairs — unconstitutional and legally challengeable.

The government launches programmes specifically aimed at fostering shared national identity and reducing ethnic tensions during a period of heightened community conflict. This is precisely what the constitutional duty of national cohesion requires.

title: Principles of national security
original_text: National security is subject to the authority of this Constitution and Parliament. National security shall be pursued in compliance with the law and with the utmost respect for the rule of law, democracy, human rights and fundamental freedoms.
what_this_means: Security forces — the military, police, intelligence services — do not have unlimited power in the name of security. Everything they do must comply with the Constitution, respect human rights, and be subject to democratic oversight. Security cannot be used as an excuse to violate rights.
examples: Intelligence officers conduct warrantless surveillance of citizens' communications, claiming national security justifies it. This is unconstitutional — national security operations must comply with the law and respect fundamental freedoms including privacy.

A security agency detains a journalist for two weeks without charge, citing national security concerns. This violates the Constitution — even security operations must respect human rights and the rule of law, including the right not to be detained without charge.

title: Establishment of national security organs
original_text: The national security organs are the Kenya Defence Forces, the National Intelligence Service, and the National Police Service. Their purpose is to protect Kenya's sovereignty, detect and prevent threats to the State, and maintain law and order respectively.
what_this_means: Kenya has three constitutionally established security bodies, each with a distinct purpose — the military defends the country, intelligence detects threats, and the police maintain order. Each body has defined boundaries and cannot lawfully act outside its constitutional mandate.
examples: The military is deployed to break up a peaceful public demonstration. Unless there is a legal declaration of a state of emergency, deploying the KDF for routine internal policing is outside their constitutional mandate — that is the role of the National Police Service.

The National Intelligence Service gathers evidence that an official is involved in corruption and arrests him using police powers. The NIS has no arrest powers — it detects threats and passes intelligence to appropriate bodies. Arrests are the mandate of the police.

title: National Security Council
original_text: There is established a National Security Council consisting of the President, Deputy President, Cabinet Secretaries for Defence, Foreign Affairs and Internal Security, and the Attorney-General. The Council exercises supervisory control over national security organs and integrates domestic, foreign and military policies on national security.
what_this_means: The National Security Council is the senior constitutional body that oversees all of Kenya's security organs. By including civilian cabinet members and the Attorney-General, the Constitution ensures that civilian and legal oversight is built into security decision-making at the highest level.
examples: Kenya faces a serious cross-border security threat involving both the military and intelligence services. The National Security Council — bringing together the President, key Cabinet Secretaries, and the Attorney-General — is the constitutionally designated body to coordinate the national response.

The NSC is convened to review intelligence reports on domestic terrorism. The Attorney-General's presence ensures that any security operations recommended are checked against legal and constitutional requirements before being authorised.

title: Kenya Defence Forces
original_text: There are established the Kenya Defence Forces consisting of the Kenya Army, Kenya Air Force and Kenya Navy. The KDF's primary mandate is to defend Kenya's sovereignty and territorial integrity. The President is Commander-in-Chief. Parliament must approve any deployment of the KDF outside Kenya.
what_this_means: The KDF exists to defend Kenya — not to police citizens. The President commands the military but cannot unilaterally send troops outside Kenya without Parliament's approval. This ensures that decisions to go to war or join foreign missions involve democratic accountability.
examples: Kenya is asked to contribute troops to an African Union peacekeeping mission in another country. Even if the President agrees, Parliament must approve the deployment of the KDF outside Kenyan territory before troops can lawfully be sent.

A county governor requests military assistance to manage post-election violence. The KDF can only be deployed internally under specific constitutional conditions — routine internal security is the mandate of the National Police Service.

title: National Intelligence Service
original_text: There is established the National Intelligence Service. It is responsible for security intelligence and counter-intelligence to enhance national security. The NIS shall not investigate ordinary crimes nor have powers of arrest.
what_this_means: The NIS is Kenya's intelligence agency — it gathers and analyses information about threats to national security. Crucially, it has no powers of arrest and cannot investigate ordinary criminal matters. It collects intelligence; it does not act as a police force or a secret police.
examples: NIS officers detain a businessman at the airport and hold him for three days for questioning about his business associates. The NIS has no powers of arrest — only the police can lawfully detain someone, and this detention is unconstitutional.

The NIS receives credible intelligence about a planned terrorist attack. Its constitutional role is to collect that intelligence and share it with the appropriate security organs — the police or KDF — who then have the authority to act on it.

title: National Police Service
original_text: There is established the National Police Service consisting of the Kenya Police Service and the Administration Police Service. The Police Service shall detect, investigate and prevent crime; maintain order and security; protect people and property; preserve peace; and enforce all laws.
what_this_means: The National Police Service is the principal law enforcement body in Kenya, with the power to arrest, investigate crime, and maintain order. It is a single national service — there is no separate county police — though counties coordinate closely with the police.
examples: A county government decides to create its own county police force separate from the National Police Service to patrol county offices. This is unconstitutional — policing is a national function, and there is only one constitutionally established police service.

A person witnesses a crime and calls the police for assistance. The National Police Service is constitutionally mandated to respond, investigate, and protect that person — this is not a favour, it is their constitutional duty.

title: Objects and functions of the National Police Service
original_text: The National Police Service shall strive to be a professional service, be non-partisan, national in character, respect human rights and dignity, and be answerable to civilian authority.
what_this_means: The police must be professional, politically neutral, and respectful of the people they serve. They are not a tool of any political party or government — they serve all Kenyans equally. And crucially, they answer to civilian oversight — not only to their commanders.
examples: Police officers are seen at political rallies in uniform, assisting in protecting only one party's gatherings while ignoring requests from opposition supporters. This partisan conduct violates the constitutional requirement that the police be non-partisan and serve all citizens equally.

A police officer brutally beats a suspect during arrest. This violates the Police Service's constitutional duty to respect human rights and dignity — the victim can sue the State and the officer can face disciplinary and criminal consequences.

title: Command of the National Police Service
original_text: There shall be an Inspector-General of the National Police Service appointed by the President with National Assembly approval. The Inspector-General has operational command of the police. The Cabinet Secretary for Internal Security may direct the Inspector-General on policy matters but not on individual investigations.
what_this_means: The Inspector-General runs day-to-day police operations independently. The Cabinet Secretary can set policy direction but cannot tell the IG how to handle any specific investigation. This protects police investigations from political interference.
examples: The Cabinet Secretary for Internal Security calls the Inspector-General and instructs him to drop an investigation into a politically connected official. The Cabinet Secretary has no constitutional authority to direct the IG on specific investigations — the IG should refuse.

The Cabinet Secretary issues a policy circular requiring all county commands to improve their crime reporting systems. This is a legitimate policy direction — the Cabinet Secretary can set policy but cannot interfere with specific operational or investigative decisions.

title: National Police Service Commission
original_text: There is established a National Police Service Commission. Its functions include: recruiting and appointing persons to positions in the Service; managing and controlling the Service; handling discipline of officers; and performing any other functions conferred by national legislation.
what_this_means: The National Police Service Commission is the oversight body that hires, manages, and disciplines police officers — independently of political control. It is designed to prevent police appointments and discipline from being driven by political connections rather than merit.
examples: A police officer is dismissed from the service following a criminal conviction. The National Police Service Commission handles such disciplinary processes — the dismissal must follow proper procedures and the officer has the right to contest it through the Commission.

A politician pressures the Inspector-General to promote a politically connected officer above more qualified candidates. The NPSC's independent role in managing promotions is designed precisely to resist this kind of interference.

title: Other security organs
original_text: Parliament may establish other security organs in addition to those established in this Constitution, subject to the national values in Article 10 and the principles of the public service in Article 232.
what_this_means: Parliament can create additional security bodies beyond the KDF, NIS, and Police — but any such bodies must adhere to constitutional values of accountability, non-discrimination, and human rights. Security bodies cannot be created as tools of oppression.
examples: Parliament creates a specialised cybersecurity agency to handle digital threats to national infrastructure. This is within Parliament's constitutional authority — but the agency must still be bound by constitutional values of accountability and human rights.

Parliament passes a law creating a parallel security agency that operates outside any oversight framework. Even though Parliament authorised it, such an agency would violate the constitutional principles that all security organs must comply with.

title: Application of the Chapter on Commissions and Independent Offices
original_text: This Chapter applies to the commissions and independent offices established by this Constitution, including the Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission, the Commission on Administrative Justice, the Kenya National Human Rights Commission, the National Cohesion and Integration Commission, the Ethics and Anti-Corruption Commission, and others.
what_this_means: Chapter 15 establishes a group of independent constitutional commissions and offices that are deliberately kept free from government control. They serve as watchdogs, enforcers of rights, and guardians of integrity — and this chapter sets out the rules that apply to all of them.
examples: A citizen wants to report human rights violations by government officials. The Kenya National Human Rights Commission is one of the independent constitutional bodies created specifically to receive and investigate such complaints — it is independent of the government being complained about.

The Ethics and Anti-Corruption Commission investigates a corrupt senior official. Because it is a constitutional commission, the government cannot lawfully interfere with or shut down the investigation — its independence is constitutionally guaranteed.

title: Objects, authority and funding of commissions
original_text: The objects of the commissions are to protect sovereignty, rights and rule of law; promote democratic values; check abuse of power; give voice to marginalised groups; and promote constitutionalism. Commissions are funded from the Consolidated Fund and are accountable to the National Assembly.
what_this_means: Constitutional commissions exist to protect ordinary citizens from abuses of power and to make sure Kenya's democratic principles are actually practised. Their funding directly from the Consolidated Fund means the government cannot starve them of resources to silence them.
examples: A government ministry tries to freeze funding to the Ethics and Anti-Corruption Commission after it opens a probe into the ministry. This is unconstitutional — commissions are funded directly from the Consolidated Fund and cannot be defunded by the executive.

A marginalised pastoral community has its concerns consistently ignored by both the national and county governments. The Kenya National Human Rights Commission is constitutionally mandated to give voice to exactly such marginalised groups and can formally investigate their complaints.

title: Membership and appointment to commissions
original_text: Each commission shall consist of not fewer than three nor more than nine members unless otherwise provided for. Appointments shall reflect Kenya's regional and ethnic diversity, be gender-balanced, and follow a transparent, competitive process involving public participation.
what_this_means: Commission appointments must represent all of Kenya — not just Nairobi or any one community. The process must be open and competitive, and gender balance is not optional. This prevents commissions from being captured by any single ethnic, regional, or gender group.
examples: The President appoints six new members of a constitutional commission but all are from his own ethnic community. This violates the constitutional requirement that appointments reflect Kenya's regional and ethnic diversity — the appointments can be legally challenged.

A commission recruitment process is conducted behind closed doors with no public advertisement or participation. The Constitution requires a transparent, competitive process involving public participation — this secretive process is unconstitutional.

title: Removal from office
original_text: A member of a commission or an independent office may be removed from office only for serious violation of the Constitution, gross misconduct, physical or mental incapacity, incompetence or neglect of duty, or bankruptcy. The process must involve a tribunal, Presidential suspension, and a parliamentary vote.
what_this_means: You cannot remove a commission member just because you disagree with them or they are inconvenient to those in power. Removal requires serious, specific grounds and a formal process involving a tribunal, presidential action, and Parliament — not a unilateral executive decision.
examples: The President is unhappy with rulings made by the chairperson of the IEBC and attempts to dismiss her by executive order. This is unconstitutional — removal requires a formal tribunal process and parliamentary approval. The chairperson can go to court to challenge the illegal dismissal.

A commission member is shown to have been taking bribes from parties they were supposed to regulate. Gross misconduct of this severity is one of the constitutionally specified grounds for removal — and the formal removal process can now be triggered.

title: General functions and powers of commissions
original_text: Each commission may investigate complaints and, in appropriate cases, take remedial action, issue summons, require production of documents, and request assistance from other state organs. A commission may also recommend legislation or administrative action.
what_this_means: Constitutional commissions are not just advisory bodies — they have teeth. They can summon people, demand documents, compel state organs to assist them, take action to remedy wrongs, and push for law or policy changes. Their findings are not easily ignored.
examples: The Commission on Administrative Justice investigates a complaint that a government ministry unreasonably denied a citizen's access to information. The Commission can summon ministry officials, demand records, and require the ministry to reverse its decision.

After investigating a series of election-related abuses, the IEBC recommends new legislation to Parliament to close legal loopholes. This is within the Commission's constitutional mandate to recommend legal reform in its area of work.

title: Commissions to be independent
original_text: In the exercise of their functions, the commissions and independent offices are independent and not subject to the direction or control of any person or authority.
what_this_means: This is one of the most important protections in the Constitution — commissions cannot be told what to do or not do by the President, Cabinet, Parliament, or any other authority. Their independence is absolute when exercising their constitutional functions.
examples: The Attorney-General instructs the Kenya National Human Rights Commission not to publish a report critical of the government's treatment of protesters. The KNCHR is constitutionally independent and the Attorney-General has no authority to suppress its reports.

A Cabinet Secretary calls the EACC to tell them to prioritise certain investigations and shelve others. The EACC cannot lawfully comply with such direction — it independently determines its own investigative priorities.

title: Reports of commissions and independent offices
original_text: As soon as practicable after the end of each financial year, each commission and independent office shall submit a report to the President and to Parliament. Each commission shall publish and publicise its reports.
what_this_means: Commissions must report annually to both the President and Parliament — and must also make those reports available to the public. This dual accountability — to the executive and the legislature — and the public disclosure requirement ensure that commissions cannot operate in secrecy.
examples: The National Cohesion and Integration Commission has not published its annual report for three consecutive years. This is a constitutional violation — the Commission is required to report annually and to publish those reports so that the public can hold it accountable.

Parliament schedules a session specifically to review the annual reports of constitutional commissions. This oversight role — enabled by the constitutional reporting requirement — allows legislators to question commission leadership about their performance and priorities.

title: Bills requiring a referendum
original_text: A proposed amendment to the Constitution shall be submitted to a referendum if it relates to: the supremacy of the Constitution; the territory of Kenya; the sovereign rights of the people; the structure of government including term limits; fundamental rights and freedoms; the independence of the Judiciary; the system of devolution; the objects of national government; or the National Land Commission.
what_this_means: Certain parts of the Constitution are so fundamental that Parliament alone cannot change them — Kenyans must vote in a referendum. Term limits for the President, the Bill of Rights, devolution, and the structure of government cannot be altered without the people's direct consent.
examples: Parliament passes a bill extending the presidential term limit from two to three terms. Because presidential term limits are a constitutionally protected matter requiring a referendum, the bill has no force until it is approved by voters in a national referendum.

A constitutional amendment is proposed to reduce the powers of county governments. Since the system of devolution is a referendum matter, this cannot be changed by Parliament alone — Kenyans must directly vote on it.

title: Bills not requiring a referendum
original_text: A Bill to amend the Constitution that does not require a referendum may be introduced in either house of Parliament and shall only become law if it is passed by both houses and, if it concerns county government matters, if approved by the majority of county assemblies.
what_this_means: Not every constitutional change requires a referendum — for matters not listed in Article 255, Parliament can amend the Constitution on its own, but only with approval from both the National Assembly and the Senate. And if the change affects devolution, county assemblies must also approve it.
examples: Parliament proposes a constitutional amendment to change the composition of a parliamentary committee structure. This is not a referendum matter — it can proceed through both Houses, but both must approve it before it becomes law.

An amendment is proposed that changes how county assemblies operate. Even if it is not a referendum matter, the majority of county assemblies must formally approve the change — they cannot have their powers altered without their own consent.

title: Popular initiative
original_text: An amendment to the Constitution may be initiated by a popular initiative supported by one million signatures from registered voters. The initiative must be considered by county assemblies and Parliament. If county assemblies and Parliament approve it, the amendment proceeds — unless it requires a referendum.
what_this_means: Ordinary Kenyans — not just politicians — can propose constitutional amendments. If you collect one million registered voter signatures, you can start a constitutional reform process. This is a direct democratic tool to change the Constitution without going through politicians.
examples: A civic group organises a popular initiative to amend the Constitution to make femicide a constitutionally recognised concern. After collecting one million signatures, the proposal must go through county assemblies and both houses of Parliament before it can become a constitutional amendment.

A million Kenyans sign a popular initiative proposing to reduce presidential term limits to one term. Because term limits are a referendum matter under Article 255, even if Parliament supports it, the change must ultimately go to a national referendum for voters to decide.

title: Right to institute proceedings
original_text: Every person has the right to institute court proceedings claiming that this Constitution has been contravened or is threatened with contravention.
what_this_means: Any Kenyan — not just lawyers or politicians — can go to court if they believe the Constitution has been broken or is about to be broken. You do not need to prove that you personally were harmed. The Constitution belongs to everyone and anyone can defend it.
examples: A student notices that Parliament is about to pass a law that conflicts with the Constitution. She can personally file a court petition — she does not need to be a lawyer or a direct victim of the law — because every Kenyan has the right to protect the Constitution.

A retired civil servant believes the government is following an unconstitutional process to amend the Constitution. He has the constitutional right to go to the High Court and challenge the process, even though he is not personally affected by the specific amendment being proposed.

title: Construing this Constitution
original_text: This Constitution shall be construed in accordance with its values and purposes. Where there is ambiguity, the interpretation that most fully promotes those values and purposes — especially the values of dignity, equality, freedom and democracy — shall be preferred. Where a right may be limited, the interpretation that limits it least shall be preferred.
what_this_means: When judges or officials interpret the Constitution, they must always choose the reading that best promotes the Constitution's purposes — especially human dignity, equality, and democracy. If there is any doubt about how much a right can be limited, the interpretation that limits it least wins.
examples: A court is interpreting an ambiguous constitutional provision about freedom of assembly. Two interpretations are possible — one that allows broad restrictions and one that allows minimal restrictions. The Constitution requires the judge to prefer the interpretation that limits the right least.

The Attorney-General interprets a constitutional clause in a way that reduces citizens' rights to access government information. If a broader, rights-affirming interpretation is equally valid, courts must prefer that interpretation — the Constitution must be read in a way that best promotes its values.

title: Interpretation of terms
original_text: In the Constitution, unless the context otherwise requires: 'children' means persons under 18; 'citizen' means a citizen of Kenya; 'county' refers to one of the 47 counties; 'disability' includes any physical, sensory, mental, psychological or other impairment; 'Parliament' means the National Assembly and the Senate together; and other defined terms apply throughout.
what_this_means: This article defines key terms used throughout the Constitution to ensure consistent meaning. Knowing these definitions matters — for example, 'Parliament' means both Houses together, not just the National Assembly, and 'disability' is defined very broadly to include mental and psychological conditions, not only physical ones.
examples: A law says 'Parliament must approve this' — a minister argues the National Assembly alone can act. This is wrong — the Constitution defines Parliament as both the National Assembly and the Senate, so both houses must be involved.

A person with a psychological condition claims they are covered by constitutional disability protections. The Constitution defines disability very broadly — including psychological impairments — so they are entitled to the same protection as someone with a physical disability.

title: Legislation required by the Constitution
original_text: Parliament shall enact all legislation required by the Constitution within the specified periods. Where no period is specified, legislation must be enacted within one year of the Constitution's effective date or, in some cases, within five years. Failure to enact required legislation may be the subject of a court application.
what_this_means: When the Constitution says Parliament shall enact a law, Parliament must actually do it — and within the timelines set. If Parliament drags its feet, any Kenyan can go to court to force Parliament to act. Constitutional obligations on Parliament are not optional.
examples: The Constitution requires Parliament to enact legislation on a specific subject within two years but five years have passed and no law has been passed. A citizen can petition the High Court to compel Parliament to enact the required legislation.

Parliament passes many of the required laws but leaves out legislation on a specific constitutional right. The persons whose rights are affected can go to court — Parliament's constitutional duty to legislate is enforceable by any citizen.

title: Transition provisions
original_text: The Schedule to this Constitution contains transitional and consequential provisions relating to the transition from the former Constitution and other laws to this Constitution.
what_this_means: When the 2010 Constitution came into force, it had to work alongside existing laws, institutions, and officeholders that were created under the old constitution. This article allows the transitional schedule to manage that handover — so the change from the old to the new constitutional order was orderly.
examples: When the 2010 Constitution came into force, sitting judges were not automatically dismissed — the transitional provisions set out a process for reviewing the continuation of their appointments. This orderly transition was enabled by the transitional schedule.

Laws that were inconsistent with the new Constitution were not all immediately void — the transitional schedule gave Parliament deadlines to amend or replace them. This prevented chaos and gave institutions time to align with the new constitutional order.

title: Date of coming into force
original_text: This Constitution shall come into force on its promulgation by the President or on the expiry of a period of 14 days from the date of the announcement of the final results of the referendum, whichever is earlier.
what_this_means: The 2010 Constitution came into force when it was formally promulgated — signed into law by the President — on 27 August 2010, following its approval in the August 2010 referendum. This article establishes the legal mechanism that gave the Constitution its force of law.
examples: After Kenyans voted overwhelmingly for the new Constitution in the August 2010 referendum, President Kibaki promulgated it at Uhuru Park on 27 August 2010. That act of promulgation gave the Constitution its legal force under this article.

Had the President not promulgated the Constitution within 14 days of the referendum results being announced, the Constitution would have automatically come into force by expiry of the 14-day period — the President could not block it by simply refusing to sign.

title: Repeal of previous Constitution
original_text: The former Constitution is repealed on the effective date of this Constitution.
what_this_means: When the 2010 Constitution came into force, it completely replaced the old 1963 Independence Constitution (as amended over the years). There is no hybrid or partial retention — the old constitution was fully repealed and the 2010 Constitution stands alone as the supreme law.
examples: After August 27, 2010, no provision of the old Kenyan constitution has any legal force. If anyone tries to rely on a provision from the old constitution to justify an action or right, they are relying on repealed law — it is the 2010 Constitution that now governs.

Lawyers and judges who were trained under the old constitution had to transition to interpreting and applying the 2010 Constitution from the date of its promulgation. The old constitution ceased to exist as a legal document on that date.
